questions.question: What is a chronic disease?
questions.answer: A chronic disease is a disease that lasts a long time (often a lifetime) and requires regular monitoring, such as diabetes, asthma, or hypertension.

questions.question: What is the difference between a viral and a bacterial infection?
questions.answer: A viral infection is caused by a virus (flu, COVID-19) and is not treated with antibiotics. A bacterial infection is caused by bacteria and may require antibiotics.

questions.question: Why does fever occur?
questions.answer: Fever is a natural bodily reaction to fight infection. It helps the immune system combat microbes.

questions.question: Diabetes is a disease where the blood sugar level is too high, either because the body does no❓ What are the symptoms of diabetes?
questions.answer: Excessive thirst, frequent urination, fatigue, unexplained weight loss, blurred vision.

questions.question: Can diabetes be cured?
questions.answer: Diabetes cannot be cured, but it can be very well controlled with treatment, a suitable diet and regular physical activity.

questions.question: What is high blood pressure?
questions.answer: It is an abnormal elevation of blood pressure in the arteries, often silent but dangerous in the long term.

questions.question: What are the risks of untreated hypertension?
questions.answer: It can cause strokes, heart attacks, kidney problems, and vision problems.

questions.question: What is asthma?
questions.answer: Asthma is a chronic respiratory disease that causes a narrowing of the bronchi, making breathing difficult.

questions.question: What are the triggers of asthma?
questions.answer: Dust, pollen, smoke, stress, cold, or respiratory infections.

questions.question: What is a stroke?
questions.answer: A stroke (cerebrovascular accident) occurs when blood flow to the brain is interrupted, causing brain damage.

questions.question: What are the warning signs of a stroke?
questions.answer: Weakness on one side of the body, slurred speech, facial paralysis, sudden loss of vision. ➡️ Immediate medical emergency.

questions.question: How is an infection transmitted?
questions.answer: Through direct contact, through air, water or contaminated food, or through infected objects.

questions.question: How to strengthen the immune system?
questions.answer: Get enough sleep, eat a balanced diet, exercise, reduce stress and avoid tobacco.

questions.question: What is gastritis?
questions.answer: It is an inflammation of the stomach lining which can cause pain, burning or nausea.

questions.question: What are the causes of abdominal pain?
questions.answer: Infections, digestive disorders, stress, unsuitable diet or chronic diseases.

questions.question: What is depression?
questions.answer: Depression is a mental illness characterized by persistent sadness, loss of interest, and constant fatigue.

questions.question: Can stress make you sick?
questions.answer: Yes, prolonged stress can cause digestive, cardiac, and sleep problems, and weaken the immune system.

questions.question: Why are regular medical check-ups important?
questions.answer: They allow diseases to be detected early, before complications arise.

questions.question: Are vaccines safe?
questions.answer: Yes, vaccines are rigorously tested and help prevent serious diseases.

questions.question: I have a fever, is it serious?
questions.answer: Fever is often a sign that the body is fighting an infection. If it exceeds 38.5°C , lasts more than 3 days , or is accompanied by significant pain, it is recommended to consult a doctor.

questions.question: I feel tired all the time, why?
questions.answer: Fatigue can be caused by stress, lack of sleep, infection, deficiency, or chronic illness.

questions.question: I feel dizzy, is it dangerous?
questions.answer: Dizziness can be related to tension, inner ear problems, stress, or dehydration. Consult a doctor if dizziness is frequent, severe, or accompanied by vision or speech problems.

questions.question: I have palpitations, should I be worried?
questions.answer: Palpitations can be linked to stress, caffeine, or fatigue. If they are frequent, painful, or associated with feeling unwell, you should consult a doctor promptly.

questions.question: How can I tell if my blood pressure is high?

questions.answer: Difficulty breathing can be caused by asthma, an infection, or anxiety. ➡️ If it appears suddenly or worsens, it is a medical emergency.

questions.question: ❓ Is a persistent cough normal?

questions.question: Can stress cause physical symptoms?

questions.question: How can I tell if I have an infection?

questions.question: When should I see a doctor?
questions.answer: It is advisable to consult a doctor if: * symptoms persist or worsen * the pain is intense * there is a high fever * there is a doubt or a concern

questions.question: Can the chatbot make a diagnosis?

The flu: sudden onset, high fever (38-40°C), intense body aches, significant fatigue, risk of complications. If fever >38.5°C + severe body aches, consult a doctor." * Q: "I've had a persistent cough for 3 weeks, is it serious?" * A: "A cough that persists for more than 3 weeks requires a consultation. Possible causes: bacterial infection, asthma, gastric reflux, allergy. Warning signs: cough with blood, shortness of breath, fever, weight loss. Consult your doctor promptly." * Q: "How can I recognize bronchitis?" * A: "Symptoms of bronchitis: chesty cough with yellow/green mucus, shortness of breath, wheezing, slight fever, fatigue. Duration 2-3 weeks. Consult if: fever >38.5°C, difficulty breathing, bloody sputum, or if you are a smoker/elderly person." Cardiovascular diseases * Q: "What are the symptoms of a heart attack?" * A: "LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY - Call 15 immediately if: you experience intense chest pain (a feeling of pressure/squeezing), pain radiating to your left arm/jaw/back, cold sweats, nausea, shortness of breath, or general malaise. Every minute counts!" * Q: "What is high blood pressure?" * A: "Hypertension = high blood pressure (≥140/90 mmHg). Often without symptoms. Risks: stroke, heart attack, kidney failure. Causes: heredity, overweight, salt, stress, sedentary lifestyle. Prevention: balanced diet, exercise, reduce salt. Regular monitoring is necessary." * Q: "What is cholesterol?" * A: "Cholesterol is an essential fat, but in excess it is dangerous. HDL (good cholesterol) protects the arteries. LDL (bad cholesterol) clogs them. High levels = cardiovascular risk. Solutions: healthy diet, physical activity, sometimes medication. Monitoring by blood test." Diabetes * Q: "What are the signs of diabetes?" * A: "Classic symptoms: intense thirst, frequent and copious urination, extreme fatigue, unexplained weight loss, blurred vision, slow wound healing, frequent infections. If these symptoms appear, have your blood sugar checked quickly. Screening by fasting blood test." * Q: "What is the difference between type 1 and type 2 diabetes?" * A: "Type 1: autoimmune disease, early onset, pancreas no longer produces insulin, lifelong insulin required. Type 2: lifestyle-related, adult onset, insulin resistance, treatable with medication/diet/exercise. Type 2 represents 90% of cases." * Q: "How can I avoid complications from diabetes?" * R: "Strict blood glucose control (target HbA1c <7%), regular monitoring (eyes, feet, kidneys), balanced diet, regular physical activity, medication intake, smoking cessation. Avoidable complications: blindness, amputation, kidney failure, heart problems." Digestive diseases * Q: "I have stomach pain after meals, why?" * A: "Common causes: food intolerance (lactose, gluten), acid reflux, irritable bowel syndrome, gastritis, gallstones. Note: which foods trigger it, type of pain, time of onset. Consult if: severe pain, vomiting, blood in the stool, weight loss." * Q: "What is irritable bowel syndrome?" * R: "Chronic digestive disorder: abdominal pain, bloating, alternating diarrhea/constipation. No organic lesion. Triggers: stress, certain foods (FODMAPs), hormones. Management: adapted diet, stress management, probiotics, medication if necessary." * Q: "What is gastroesophageal reflux?" * A: "Acid reflux from the stomach into the esophagus. Symptoms: heartburn, acidic taste, nighttime cough, hoarseness. Causes: overweight, heavy meals, tobacco, alcohol. Solutions: lose weight, avoid fatty/spicy foods, elevate the head of the bed, antacids." Dermatological diseases * Q: "How can you tell the difference between eczema and psoriasis?" * A: "Eczema: red, oozing patches, intense itching, localized to areas such as skin folds and hands. Psoriasis: thick patches with silvery scales, localized to areas such as elbows, knees, and scalp, with moderate itching. Both are chronic, non-contagious, treatable but not curable." * Q: "Is it dangerous if a mole changes?" * R: "CONSULT QUICKLY if ABCDE rule: A=Asymmetry, B=Irregular borders, C=Uneven color, D=Diameter >6mm, E=Change (size, shape, color). Other warning signs: bleeding, itching, new growth after age 40. Early detection = effective treatment." Neurological diseases * Q: "What are the signs of a stroke?" * R: "ABSOLUTE EMERGENCY - Call 15 if: one side of your face is paralyzed, your arm is weak/numb, you have slurred speech (unintelligible words), you lose your vision, you experience severe vertigo, or you have a severe headache. The FAST method: Face-Disability-Slurred Speech-Extreme Emergency. Every minute = neurons lost!" * Q: "What is a migraine?" * A: "Intense, throbbing headache, often on one side only, lasting 4-72 hours. Associated symptoms: nausea, sensitivity to light/noise, sometimes visual aura. Triggers: stress, food, hormones, lack of sleep. Treatments: analgesics, triptans, prevention if attacks are frequent." * Q: "Alzheimer's, what are the first signs?" * R: "Early signs: frequent forgetfulness (recent events), difficulty planning, time/spatial confusion, language problems, misplacing objects, mood changes, social withdrawal. Distinguish from normal aging. Early diagnosis is important to slow progression." Infectious diseases * Q: "How can I tell if it's viral or bacterial tonsillitis?" * A: "Impossible to differentiate without a test. General signs: red throat, pain when swallowing, fever. Rapid test in 5 minutes at the doctor's office. Viral (80%): no antibiotics. Bacterial (streptococcus): antibiotics necessary to avoid complications (rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis)." * Q: "What are the symptoms of mononucleosis?" * R: "Kissing disease: extreme fatigue (several weeks), severe sore throat, fever, swollen lymph nodes (neck), sometimes enlarged spleen. Diagnosis: blood test. Treatment: complete rest, hydration, pain relievers. Avoid contact sports (risk of splenic rupture). Contagious through saliva." * Q: "Tuberculosis still exists?" * A: "Yes, 10 million cases/year worldwide. Symptoms: persistent cough >3 weeks, bloody sputum, fever, night sweats, weight loss. Contagious through the air. Screening: chest x-ray, skin test. Long treatment (6-9 months) but effective if followed correctly." Rheumatic diseases * Q: "Osteoarthritis or arthritis, what's the difference?" * A: "Osteoarthritis: cartilage wear, mechanical pain (exertion), morning stiffness <30min, after age 50, knees/hips/hands. Arthritis: joint inflammation, inflammatory pain (rest/night), stiffness >1h, swelling/heat, any age. Different treatments." * Q: "What is rheumatoid arthritis?" * A: "Autoimmune disease: the immune system attacks the joints. Symptoms: joint pain/swelling (hands, wrists), morning stiffness lasting more than 1 hour, fatigue, symmetrical. Untreated = deformities. Early treatment (immunosuppressants) prevents disability. Diagnosis by blood test + X-ray." Thyroid diseases * Q: "Symptoms of a thyroid problem?" * A: "Hypothyroidism (slowed down): fatigue, weight gain, feeling cold, constipation, dry skin, depression. Hyperthyroidism (accelerated): weight loss, nervousness, palpitations, sweating, tremors, insomnia. Diagnosis: blood test (TSH). Effective drug treatment." Chronic respiratory diseases * Q: "What is COPD?" * R: "Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease: progressive destruction of the lungs, mainly by tobacco. Symptoms: progressive shortness of breath, chronic cough, sputum production, frequent infections. Irreversible but progression slowed by smoking cessation, medication, respiratory rehabilitation." * Q: "How to recognize asthma?" * A: "Attacks of: shortness of breath, wheezing, chest tightness, cough (especially at night). Triggers: allergens, exertion, cold air, stress, infections. Diagnosis: breathing test (spirometry). Treatment: inhalers (emergency + maintenance). Well controlled = normal life." Urinary and Kidney Diseases * Q: "What is a urinary tract infection?" * A: "Bacterial bladder infection (cystitis). Symptoms: burning sensation when urinating, frequent and urgent urges, cloudy/smelly urine, sometimes blood. More common in women. Treatment: antibiotics for 3-7 days. Drink plenty of water. Consult a doctor if fever/lower back pain (possible kidney infection)." * Q: "Kidney stones, how do they manifest themselves?" * R: "Renal colic: intense and sudden pain (back/side towards groin), nausea/vomiting, blood in the urine, restlessness. Pain comes in waves. Causes: dehydration, diet high in salt/protein. Prevention: drink 2L of water/day. Treatment: strong painkillers, sometimes intervention." * Q: "What is kidney failure?" * A: "The kidneys are no longer filtering the blood properly. Acute (sudden): infection, dehydration, medication. Chronic (progressive): diabetes, hypertension, kidney disease. Late symptoms: fatigue, edema, shortness of breath, nausea. Screening: blood creatinine. Advanced stages = dialysis/transplant." * Q: "Why do I urinate so much at night?" * A: "Common causes: diabetes (check blood sugar), prostate problems (men >50 years old), heart failure, drinking too much in the evening, diuretic medications, urinary tract infection, aging bladder. If >2 times/night regularly, consult for evaluation." Liver Diseases * Q: "What is cirrhosis?" * R: "Progressive and irreversible destruction of the liver forming scars. Causes: alcohol (main), hepatitis B/C, steatosis (fatty liver). Silent onset, then: fatigue, jaundice, ascites (swollen abdomen), bleeding, confusion. Serious complications: liver cancer, hepatic coma. Stopping alcohol is essential." * Q: "Hepatitis A, B, C: differences?" * A: "Hepatitis A: fecal-oral transmission (water/food), complete cure, vaccine exists. Hepatitis B: blood/sexual transmission, chronic 5-10%, vaccine exists, lifelong treatment. Hepatitis C: blood transmission, chronic 75%, no vaccine, CURE possible with new drugs. Common symptoms: fatigue, jaundice, nausea." * Q: "What is hepatic steatosis?" * A: "Non-alcoholic fatty liver: accumulation of fat in the liver. Causes: overweight, diabetes, cholesterol, sedentary lifestyle. Often asymptomatic. Risk: progression to cirrhosis. Diagnosis: ultrasound, blood test. Treatment: weight loss, exercise, balanced diet. Reversible if caught early." * Q: "Is jaundice serious?" * A: "Jaundice = yellowing of the skin/eyes due to bilirubin accumulation. Causes: hepatitis, cirrhosis, gallstones, pancreas, red blood cell destruction, newborn (normal). ALWAYS consult a doctor quickly. Diagnosis by liver function tests. Severity depends on the underlying cause." Gynecological Diseases * Q: "What is endometriosis?" * A: "Uterine tissue develops outside the uterus (ovaries, fallopian tubes, peritoneum). Symptoms: very painful periods, chronic pelvic pain, pain during intercourse/defecation, possible infertility. Difficult diagnosis (ultrasound, MRI, laparoscopy). Treatment: painkillers, hormones, surgery. Chronic disease requiring monitoring." * Q: "What is Polycystic Ovary Syndrome?" * A: "PCOS: hormonal imbalance. Symptoms: irregular/absent periods, acne, excessive hair growth, weight gain, ovarian cysts, infertility. Common cause of infertility. Risks: diabetes, heart disease. Treatment: birth control pill, metformin, weight loss, fertility treatment if pregnancy is desired." * Q: "Are uterine fibroids dangerous?" * A: "Benign (non-cancerous) uterine tumors, very common after age 30. Often asymptomatic. Possible symptoms: heavy/prolonged periods, pelvic pain, heaviness, bladder/rectal compression, rare infertility. Monitoring or treatment (medication, embolization, surgery) depending on symptoms." * Q: "What is a chlamydia infection?" * A: "Very common bacterial STI, often silent (70% of women are asymptomatic). Symptoms: abnormal vaginal discharge, burning during urination, bleeding, pelvic pain. Untreated: infertility, ectopic pregnancy. Simple screening (urine/smear). Treatment: effective antibiotics. Treat partner as well." Hematological Diseases * Q: "Anemia, what are the symptoms?" * A: "Lack of red blood cells/hemoglobin. Symptoms: intense fatigue, paleness, shortness of breath, dizziness, palpitations, headaches, brittle nails. Causes: iron deficiency (menstruation, pregnancy), B12/folate deficiency, bleeding, chronic diseases, genetics. Diagnosis: blood test. Treatment according to cause." * Q: "Leukemia, how can it be detected?" * A: "White blood cell cancer. Symptoms: extreme fatigue, frequent infections, easy bleeding/bruising, persistent fever, swollen lymph nodes, bone pain, paleness, weight loss. Diagnosis: blood test (abnormal differential), bone marrow aspiration. Treatments: chemotherapy, bone marrow transplant. Prognosis varies depending on type." * Q: "What is sickle cell disease?" * R: "Genetic disease of red blood cells (sickle shape). Primarily of African origin. Symptoms: intense painful attacks (bones, abdomen), anemia, frequent infections, jaundice, growth retardation. Complications: stroke, lungs, spleen. Treatment: hydration, analgesics, transfusions, hydroxyurea. Neonatal screening." * Q: "What is hemophilia?" * A: "Genetic disease: blood does not clot normally. Hereditary, X-linked (mostly boys). Symptoms: prolonged bleeding, spontaneous bruising, joint bleeding (pain, disability). Variable severity. Treatment: coagulation factor injection (preventive or as needed). Avoid contact sports." Ophthalmic Diseases * Q: "What is a cataract?" * A: "Opacification of the lens (eye's lens). Related to aging (after 60 years). Progressive symptoms: blurred/cloudy vision, glare, dull colors, difficulty seeing at night, double vision. Treatment: surgery (very effective, outpatient). The only effective treatment. A common and safe operation." * Q: "Glaucoma, why is it serious?" * A: "High eye pressure destroys the optic nerve. Silent at first! Progressive, irreversible loss of peripheral vision. Untreated = blindness. Screening: pressure measurement + fundus examination after age 40. Risk factors: age, heredity, high myopia. Treatment: daily eye drops, laser, surgery. Lifelong follow-up." * Q: "What is AMD?" * A: "Age-Related Macular Degeneration: destruction of the central part of the retina (macula). After age 50. Symptoms: blurred central vision, distorted lines, central dark spot, difficulty reading/recognizing faces. Dry form (90%): slow. Wet form (10%): rapid. Treatment: intravitreal injections, vitamins. Screening using the Amsler grid." * Q: "Viral or bacterial conjunctivitis?" * A: "Conjunctivitis (white of the eye). Viral: red eye, clear tearing, itching, contagious, spontaneous healing in 1-2 weeks. Bacterial: thick yellow/green secretions, eyelids stuck together in the morning, topical antibiotics. Allergic: intense itching, seasonal, antihistamines. Saline irrigation is always helpful." ENT diseases * Q: "How can I recognize sinusitis?" * R: "Sinus inflammation (facial cavities). Symptoms: facial pain (forehead, cheeks, between the eyes), worse when bending over, stuffy nose, thick yellow/green discharge, headache, sometimes fever. Acute: post-cold, viral then sometimes bacterial. Chronic: >3 months, allergic/anatomical predisposition. Antibiotics if prolonged bacterial infection." * Q: "Otitis, adult or child different?" * A: "Child: very common (short fallopian tubes), acute otitis media, intense pain, fever, crying, sometimes perforated eardrum. Adult: less common, often otitis externa (swimming), pain on pressure in the ear. Treatment: analgesics, antibiotics if superinfection. Rare but serious complications if left untreated." * Q: "What causes dizziness?" * R: "Rotational vertigo: sensation of rotation (of yourself or the environment). Causes: BPPV (inner ear crystals, the most common), Ménière's disease (vertigo + deafness + tinnitus), vestibular neuritis, migraine. Instability: neurological, cardiovascular, or medication-related causes. Consult a doctor if you experience: intense vertigo, deafness, headaches, or neurological signs." * Q: "Is tinnitus treatable?" * A: "Parasite noises (whistling, buzzing) without an external source. Causes: noise exposure, aging, medications, Ménière's disease, earwax buildup. Often without curative treatment. Management: sound therapy, CBT, relaxation, hearing aids, ear protection. Consult a doctor to rule out a treatable cause (wax buildup, infection, rare tumor)." Dental Diseases * Q: "How does tooth decay occur?" * R: "Tooth destruction by bacteria and sugars. Stages: white spot (reversible), enamel cavity (painless), dentin involvement (sensitivity), pulp involvement (toothache), abscess. Prevention: brushing twice a day, flossing, limiting sugars and fluoride, annual scaling. Treatment: filling, root canal, crown depending on the stage." * Q: "Gingivitis or periodontitis?" * A: "Gingivitis: inflammation of the gums (red, swollen, bleeds when brushing), reversible, caused by dental plaque. Periodontitis: advanced stage, destruction of bone/ligaments, receding gums, pockets, abscesses, tooth loss. Factors: tobacco, diabetes, stress. Treatment: scaling, root planing, sometimes surgery. Rigorous hygiene is essential." * Q: "Dental abscess, what to do?" * A: "Bacterial infection with pus. Symptoms: intense throbbing pain, gum/cheek swelling, fever, bad taste, swollen lymph node in the neck. EMERGENCY: see a dentist quickly. Risk: spread of infection (heart, brain). Treatment: drainage, antibiotics, treat the tooth (root canal/extraction). In the meantime: pain relievers, mouthwash, cold." Bone Diseases * Q: "Osteoporosis, who is at risk?" * A: "Bone fragility (porous bone). Risks: postmenopausal women, age >65 years, family history, long-term corticosteroid use, thinness, smoking, alcohol, sedentary lifestyle. Silent until fracture (wrist, vertebrae, hip). Prevention: calcium, vitamin D, exercise, smoking cessation. Screening: bone densitometry. Treatment: bisphosphonates." * Q: "What is a stress fracture?" * R: "Microfracture due to repeated stress without trauma. Athletes, military personnel, osteoporosis. Locations: metatarsus (foot), tibia, fibula. Symptoms: progressive pain with exertion, decreases with rest, swelling. Diagnosis: X-ray (normal at the beginning), MRI, bone scan. Treatment: rest for 6-8 weeks, generally no cast." * Q: "Paget's disease of the bones?" * R: "Disorganized bone remodeling: thickened, deformed, fragile bones. After age 50. Often asymptomatic (incidental discovery). Symptoms: bone pain, deformities (skull, legs), fractures, osteoarthritis, nerve compression, deafness. Complications: heart failure, exceptionally cancer. Treatment: bisphosphonates if symptomatic." Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) * Q: "HIV/AIDS, what's the difference?" * A: "HIV: virus destroys the immune system. Transmission: blood, sexual intercourse, mother-to-child. Asymptomatic phase: years. AIDS: advanced stage of HIV, very low defenses, opportunistic infections, cancers. Screening: blood test (3 months after risk). Treatment: triple therapy (undetectable viral load = not transmissible). No cure but normal life." * Q: "Does syphilis still exist?" * R: "Yes, resurgence! Bacterial STI. Stage 1: painless chancre in contact area (disappears on its own). Stage 2: rash on body/palms/soles, lymph nodes. Latency for years. Stage 3: serious damage (heart, brain, eyes). Blood screening. Treatment: penicillin is very effective. Treat partners. Can cross the placenta (congenital syphilis)." * Q: "Is genital herpes curable?" * A: "Not curable, the virus remains for life. Transmission: skin/mucous membrane contact, sexual intercourse, even without symptoms. Primary infection: painful blisters, fever, swollen lymph nodes. Recurrences (stress, fatigue, menstruation): less severe. Treatment: antivirals (shorten the outbreak, reduce recurrences). Contagious even without visible symptoms. Condoms reduce the risk." * Q: "What is Human Papillomavirus (HPV)?" * A: "Very common virus, sexually transmitted. 80% of the population is infected at some point in their lives. Often asymptomatic, cleared naturally. Some types: genital warts (benign). High-risk types: cervical, anal, and throat cancer. Screening: Pap smear. Prevention: vaccine (girls and boys 11-14 years old), partial condoms. Treatment for warts, monitoring for precancerous lesions." Autoimmune Diseases * Q: "What exactly is Lupus?" * R: "Systemic autoimmune disease: the body attacks its own organs. Mostly affects young women. Variable symptoms: extreme fatigue, fever, facial rash (wolf mask), photosensitivity, joint pain, kidney/heart/lung/brain involvement. Diagnosis: antinuclear antibodies. Treatments: corticosteroids, immunosuppressants. Course in flare-ups. Improved prognosis." * Q: "Multiple sclerosis, initial symptoms?" * R: "Autoimmune disease of the central nervous system. Onset between 20 and 40 years of age. Variable symptoms (depending on affected areas): visual disturbances (optic neuritis is common), numbness/tingling, limb weakness, balance/gait disturbances, fatigue, urinary problems. Unpredictable course (flare-ups-remissions or progressive). Diagnosis: MRI, lumbar puncture. Treatments slow the progression." * Q: "What is Crohn's disease?" * R: "Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), autoimmune. Location: mainly ileum/colon. Symptoms: chronic diarrhea, abdominal pain, weight loss, fatigue, sometimes fever, fistulas, abscesses. Course in flare-ups. Complications: obstruction, perforation, malnutrition. Diagnosis: colonoscopy, MRI. Treatment: anti-inflammatories, immunosuppressants, biotherapies. Incurable but controllable." * Q: "Celiac disease (gluten intolerance)?" * R: "Autoimmune disease triggered by gluten. Intestinal attack, malabsorption. Symptoms: diarrhea, bloating, pain, fatigue, weight loss, deficiencies (iron, calcium, vitamins), growth retardation in children. Sometimes asymptomatic. Diagnosis: serology, intestinal biopsy. Treatment: STRICT gluten-free diet for life. Mucosal healing with adherence." Mental Illness * Q: "Depression, what are the warning signs?" * R: "Persistent sadness >2 weeks, loss of interest/pleasure in activities, extreme fatigue, sleep disturbances (insomnia or hypersomnia), altered appetite, difficulty concentrating, feelings of worthlessness/guilt, thoughts of death. EMERGENCY if suicidal thoughts: 3114 (suicide prevention number). Treatment: psychotherapy + antidepressants if severe. Curable with appropriate help." * Q: "What is bipolar disorder?" * R: "Alternating depressive and manic/hypomanic phases. Depression: sadness, slowing down. Mania: euphoria/irritability, excessive energy, insomnia without fatigue, grandiose ideas, risky behaviors, excessive spending. Hypomania: attenuated version. Diagnosis often delayed. Treatment: mood stabilizers (lithium), antipsychotics, psychotherapy. Lifelong follow-up." * Q: "Schizophrenia, symptoms?" * R: "Chronic psychotic illness. Positive symptoms: hallucinations (frequent auditory), delusions, disorganized thinking/behavior. Negative symptoms: social withdrawal, emotional blunting, apathy, impoverished language. Begins in young adulthood. Causes: genetics + environment. Treatment: antipsychotics, psychosocial rehabilitation. Remissions are possible with treatment." * Q: "Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD)?" * R: "Excessive and uncontrollable anxiety about daily events, >6 months. Symptoms: persistent worry, muscle tension, fatigue, irritability, concentration/sleep disturbances, physical symptoms (palpitations, sweating, trembling). Different from normal stress. Treatment: CBT (very effective), SSRI antidepressants, relaxation techniques, exercise." * Q: "OCD (obsessive-compulsive disorder)?" * R: "Obsessions: recurring intrusive and distressing thoughts (contamination, doubt, order, forbidden thoughts). Compulsions: repetitive behaviors to reduce anxiety (hand washing, checking, rituals). Intrusive daily life. Person aware of absurdity but cannot stop. Treatment: CBT (exposure with prevention and response) + SSRIs. Significant improvement possible." Specific Pediatric Diseases * Q: "What is roseola infantum?" * R: "Benign viral illness, 6-24 months. Sudden high fever for 3-4 days, child paradoxically otherwise well. Then pink rash appears on the body when the fever subsides. Diagnosis: clinical. Treatment: antipyretics. Spontaneous recovery. Rare complications (febrile seizures). Lifelong immunity afterward." * Q: "Is hand, foot, and mouth disease contagious?" * A: "Viral infection in children <5 years old. Highly contagious (saliva, feces). Symptoms: slight fever, painful blisters in the mouth, rash on hands/feet/buttocks. Mouth pain → difficulty eating. Duration 7-10 days. Treatment: symptomatic (pain), cold/liquid foods. Exclusion from daycare until lesions heal. Generally benign." * Q: "Whooping cough, still dangerous?" * A: "Highly contagious bacterial infection. Infant: SEVERE (apnea, respiratory distress, possible death). Child/adult: persistent cough for 2-3 months (violent coughing fits, 'rooster crow' inspiratory arrest), post-cough vomiting. Diagnosis: swab. Treatment: antibiotics. Prevention: essential vaccination (adult boosters to protect infants)." * Q: "Is scarlet fever still common?" * A: "Streptococcal bacterial infection (strep throat + toxin). Children 5-10 years old. Symptoms: fever, red sore throat, strawberry tongue, rough red rash (body, skin folds), secondary desquamation. Contagious. Diagnosis: strep throat test. Treatment: mandatory antibiotics for 10 days (otherwise cardiac/renal complications). Exclusion from school 48 hours after antibiotics." Cancers

questions.question: Breast cancer, how to detect it?
questions.answer: Monthly self-examination: look for lumps/thickening, changes in breast shape/size, nipple retraction, discharge, orange peel skin, and axillary lymph nodes. Mammography: every 2 years for ages 50-74 (organized screening), earlier if there is a family history. Warning signs: hard, painless lump, recent change. Early detection = 90% cure rate. Risk factors: age, heredity, hormones.

questions.question: Colorectal cancer, at what age should screening begin?
questions.answer: Screening for ages 50-74: stool immunochemical test every 2 years (looks for invisible blood). Symptoms: blood in stool, changes in bowel habits (diarrhea/constipation), abdominal pain, weight loss, anemia. Risk factors: age, family history, IBD, polyps. Colonoscopy if test is positive. Early detection: 90% cure rate. Prevention: fiber, limit red meat/processed meats.

questions.question: Prostate cancer, how can I tell?
questions.answer: Common after age 50. Often asymptomatic at the beginning. Late symptoms: urinary difficulties (weak stream, dribbling, frequent urges at night), blood in urine/semen, bone pain (metastases). Screening is controversial: PSA test + digital rectal exam. Progression is often slow. Active surveillance is possible if it is not very aggressive. Treatments: surgery, radiotherapy, hormone therapy.

questions.question: Lymphoma, what are the symptoms?
questions.answer: Lymph node cancer. Hodgkin (young adults) vs. Non-Hodgkin (older adults). Symptoms: painless swollen lymph nodes (neck/armpits/groin) >1 month, unexplained fever, profuse night sweats, unintentional weight loss, fatigue, generalized itching. Diagnosis: lymph node biopsy. Treatments: chemotherapy, immunotherapy. High cure rate, especially for Hodgkin.

questions.question: Ovarian cancer, difficult to detect?
questions.answer: Yes, 'silent killer'. Vague late symptoms: persistent bloating, pelvic/abdominal pain, rapid satiety, urinary problems, changes in bowel habits. Risks: age, family history (BRCA mutation), nulliparity, endometriosis. No effective screening. Diagnosis: ultrasound, CA-125, biopsy. Treatment: surgery + chemotherapy. Prognosis better if early stage.

questions.question: Childhood leukemia, unusual signs?
questions.answer: Blood cancer is more common in children. Symptoms: unusual extreme fatigue, paleness, persistent fever, repeated infections, abnormal bleeding/bruising, bone/joint pain, swollen lymph nodes/spleen/liver, loss of appetite/weight. The child refuses to play/walk. Diagnosis: urgent blood test. Prognosis in children is better than in adults: 80-90% cure rate with current treatments.

questions.question: Thyroid cancer, good prognosis?
questions.answer: Generally YES, excellent prognosis. Types: papillary (85%, best prognosis), follicular, medullary, anaplastic (rare, aggressive). Symptoms: palpable thyroid nodule, dysphonia, dysphagia, swollen neck lymph nodes. Diagnosis: ultrasound, fine-needle aspiration, scintigraphy. Treatment: surgery + radioactive iodine. Survival >95% at 10 years for differentiated types. Lifelong monitoring. Metabolic Diseases

questions.question: What exactly is a drop?
questions.answer: Excess uric acid crystallizes in the joints. Acute attack: sudden, intense pain (often in the big toe), red, hot, swollen, at night. Impossible to touch/walk. Factors: rich diet (red meat, offal, seafood, alcohol, sodas), obesity, genetics, kidney failure. Treatment of an attack: anti-inflammatories. Prevention: allopurinol, diet, hydration. Untreated: tophi, joint destruction.

questions.question: Hemochromatosis, a French genetic disease?
questions.answer: Genetic iron overload, common in the European population (1/300). Excessive intestinal iron absorption. Late symptoms: chronic fatigue, joint pain, skin pigmentation (tanned), liver/heart/pancreas damage, diabetes, impotence. Diagnosis: elevated ferritin + transferrin saturation, genetic testing. Simple treatment: regular phlebotomy. Early detection avoids complications.

questions.question: Phenylketonuria, neonatal screening?
questions.answer: Genetic metabolic disorder. Inability to break down phenylalanine (amino acid). Toxic accumulation in the brain. Systematic screening at birth (Guthrie test). Untreated: severe intellectual disability, epilepsy, behavioral disorders. Treatment: strict lifelong low-phenylalanine diet (avoid animal proteins, aspartame). Follow-up if treated: normal development. Genetic counseling during pregnancy. Tropical and Infectious Diseases

questions.question: Malaria, risk areas?
questions.answer: Parasitic disease transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes. Areas: Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, South America. Symptoms 7-30 days after bite: cyclical fever, severe chills, sweats, headache, nausea, fatigue. Severe form: cerebral involvement, kidney failure, death. Travel prevention: preventive treatment, mosquito net, repellent. Diagnosis: thick blood smear. Urgent treatment required.

questions.question: Dengue, symptoms?
questions.answer: Virus transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes (daytime). Tropical areas, worldwide spread. Incubation 4-7 days. Symptoms: sudden high fever, intense headache/eye pain, muscle/joint pain ('bone-breaking fever'), rash, bleeding. Severe form: hemorrhaging, shock. No specific treatment: rest, hydration, paracetamol (NOT aspirin). Prevention: mosquito repellent, eliminate stagnant water. Vaccine exists in some countries.

questions.question: Lyme disease, tick = danger?
questions.answer: Bacterium transmitted by an infected tick (not all). Forested/grassy areas. Phase 1: erythema migrans (spreading red rash, clear center), 3-30 days after bite. Phase 2 (weeks/months): neurological, cardiac, and joint involvement. Phase 3 (months/years): chronic arthritis, neurological involvement. Difficult diagnosis. Treatment: antibiotics are effective if treated early. Prevention: long clothing, post-walk inspection, and rapid tick removal.

questions.question: What is typhoid fever?
questions.answer: Bacterial infection (Salmonella typhi) through contaminated water/food. Developing countries. Incubation 1-3 weeks. Symptoms: fever progressing to a plateau, headache, constipation then diarrhea, abdominal pain, confusion, roseola rash. Complications: intestinal perforation, hemorrhage. Diagnosis: blood culture. Treatment: antibiotics. Travel prevention: vaccination, strict food hygiene.

questions.question: Bilharziasis (schistosomiasis)?
questions.answer: Flatworm parasitosis, transmission via contaminated freshwater (Africa, Middle East, South America, Asia). Skin penetration occurs during bathing. Acute phase: dermatitis, fever (Katayama fever). Chronic phase: bladder involvement (blood in urine, cancer), intestinal/liver involvement (bloody diarrhea, cirrhosis). Diagnosis: stool/urine examination, serology. Treatment: praziquantel is effective. Prevention: avoid swimming in freshwater in endemic areas.

questions.question: Rage, always deadly?
questions.answer: Virus transmitted by bite/scratch from an infected animal (dog, bat, fox). Variable incubation period (weeks to months). Symptoms: fever, anxiety, hydrophobia (fear of water), aerophobia, hypersalivation, agitation, paralysis. FATAL once symptoms appear (>99.9%). Prevention: preventive vaccination for travel at risk, URGENT post-exposure treatment (washing + vaccine + immunoglobulins). Any bite from a suspected animal = medical emergency.

questions.question: Chikungunya, after-effects?
questions.answer: Virus transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes (same as dengue). Tropical areas + Southern France. Symptoms: sudden fever, INTENSE joint pain (knees, wrists, ankles), rash. Not fatal but persistent joint pain for months/years (30-40% of cases). No specific treatment. Diagnosis: serology. Prevention: mosquito repellent. Name means 'one who walks bent over' in Swahili. Medical Emergencies

questions.question: Appendicitis, how can I be sure?
questions.answer: Appendix inflammation. Classic symptoms: pain starting around the navel then in the right iliac fossa, worsened by coughing/walking, nausea/vomiting, loss of appetite, moderate fever, constipation. Blumberg sign: pain on release of abdominal pressure. Consult the emergency room if suspected. Diagnosis: clinical examination + ultrasound/CT scan. Treatment: surgery (emergency if peritonitis). Not appendicitis = left-sided pain.

questions.question: Intestinal obstruction, signs?
questions.answer: Intestinal transit blockage. Causes: post-surgical adhesions, strangulated hernia, tumor, volvulus. Symptoms: cessation of gas/stool passage, abdominal pain, cramps, bloating, vomiting (late = fecaloid), dehydration. Distended abdomen, metallic bowel sounds followed by silence. Surgical EMERGENCY. Diagnosis: abdominal X-ray/CT scan. Treatment: feeding tube, rehydration, surgery if necessary.

questions.question: Meningitis, how to recognize an emergency?
questions.answer: Meningitis (bacterial/viral infection). EMERGENCY if bacterial. Symptoms: high fever, severe headache, stiff neck (cannot touch chin-chest), photophobia, vomiting, confusion, purpura (purple spots that do not disappear with pressure). Infant: bulging fontanelle, high-pitched cry, drowsiness. Call 15 if suspected. Diagnosis: lumbar puncture. Urgent antibiotic treatment. Vaccines available.

questions.question: SURGICAL EMERGENCY <6h. Spermatic cord twists, severing testicular vascular supply. Symptoms: SUDDEN, INTENSE scrotal pain, testicle retracted horizontally, nausea/vomiting, absence of cremasteric reflex. Primarily affects adolescents/young adults. Diagnosis: clinical examination + Doppler ultrasound. Urgent surgery for detorsion and fixation. Delay >6h = testicular loss (necrosis). Any sudden testicular pain = emergency.
questions.answer: Rare Diseases

questions.question: Is Huntington's disease hereditary?
questions.answer: Neurodegenerative genetic disease. Autosomal dominant transmission (50% risk if affected parent). Onset 30-50 years. Symptoms: involuntary movements (chorea), psychiatric disorders (depression, irritability), progressive cognitive decline. Inevitable progression to dementia and disability. No curative treatment. Predictive genetic testing possible (difficult decision). Genetic counseling important. Life expectancy reduced 15-20 years after onset.

questions.question: Myasthenia gravis, muscle weakness?
questions.answer: Autoimmune disease: antibodies block nerve-muscle junction receptors. Symptoms: fluctuating muscle weakness worsened by exertion/end of day. Typical features: drooping eyelids (ptosis), double vision (diplopia), swallowing/chewing difficulties, nasal voice, limb weakness. Myasthenic crisis: respiratory distress (emergency). Diagnosis: Tensilon test, electromyogram, antibodies. Treatment: anticholinesterases, immunosuppressants, thymectomy. Good prognosis with treatment.

questions.question: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP)?
questions.answer: Autoimmune disease: destruction of platelets. Child (post-viral infection, spontaneous recovery) or adult (chronic). Symptoms: purpura (red/blue spots on the skin), easy bruising, nosebleeds/gums, heavy periods. Severity if platelets <10,000 (risk of cerebral hemorrhage). Diagnosis: blood test (isolated low platelets). Treatment if severe: corticosteroids, immunoglobulins, splenectomy. Regular monitoring. Allergies and Intolerances * Q: "Food allergy vs. intolerance, what's the difference?" * A: "Allergy: an immune system reaction, can be severe (anaphylaxis), occurs rapidly (within minutes), even a small amount can trigger it. Common allergens: peanuts, milk, eggs, tree nuts, fish, wheat, soy. Intolerance: a digestive reaction without immunity, dose-dependent, less severe. Examples: lactose, gluten (celiac disease = autoimmune allergy). Allergy diagnosis: skin tests, specific IgE. Treatment: strict avoidance, antihistamines, adrenaline (emergency)." * Q: "Anaphylactic shock, what to do?" * R: "LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY. Generalized allergic reaction: giant hives, facial/throat swelling, shortness of breath, hypotension, loss of consciousness. Death possible within minutes. Call 15 immediately. If an epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) is available: inject into the outer thigh, keep lying down with legs elevated. Repeat injection if there is no improvement in 5-15 minutes. Go to the emergency room even if there is improvement. Causes: food, medication, insect bites." * Q: "Pollen allergy, how to manage it?" * A: "Seasonal allergic rhinitis. Symptoms: bouts of sneezing, runny/blocked nose, red/watery eyes, itching. Seasons: trees (February-April), grasses (May-July), herbaceous plants (August-October). Management: follow pollen calendar, ventilate early in the morning, sunglasses, antihistamines, topical corticosteroids. Desensitization: effective maintenance treatment (3-5 years). Complication: allergic asthma." * Q: "Hives, common causes?" * A: "Raised red patches, intense itching, fleeting (<24h). Causes: allergies (food, medication, latex), viral infections, stress, cold/heat/pressure, autoimmune diseases, idiopathic (50%). Acute (<6 weeks): often allergic. Chronic (>6 weeks): often idiopathic. Treatment: antihistamines, avoidance of triggering factor. Emergency if throat/face swelling (Quincke's edema)." * Q: "Drug allergy, how to react?" * A: "Symptoms: skin rash, hives, swelling, fever, difficulty breathing. Common medications: penicillin, aspirin, NSAIDs, contrast agents. Stop the suspected medication immediately. Consult a doctor. Carefully note the responsible medication (allergy bracelet). Stevens-Johnson syndrome (skin detachment) and DRESS (organ involvement) are serious dermatological emergencies. Always disclose known allergies before prescribing." * Q: "Dust mite allergy, how to reduce exposure?" * A: "Dust mites = major indoor allergens. Live in bedding, carpets, and soft toys. Permanent symptoms: rhinitis, asthma, conjunctivitis. Measures: anti-dust mite mattress/pillow covers, weekly washing of bedding at 60°C, daily ventilation, vacuum cleaner with HEPA filter, avoid heavy carpets/curtains, humidity <50%. Antihistamines, nasal corticosteroids. Subcutaneous/sublingual desensitization." Nutrition and Dietary Diseases * Q: "Obesity, illness or lifestyle choice?" * A: "Obesity IS a complex chronic disease. BMI >30kg/m². Multifactorial causes: genetics, hormones, microbiota, environment, psychology, socioeconomics. Not simply a lack of willpower. Complications: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, sleep apnea, osteoarthritis, cancers, depression, PCOS. Treatments: nutritional rehabilitation, physical activity, psychotherapy, medication, bariatric surgery if BMI >40." * Q: "Anorexia nervosa, signs to recognize?" * R: "Severe eating disorder. Refusal to maintain normal weight, intense fear of gaining weight, distorted body image. Signs: extreme weight loss, severe food restriction, physical hyperactivity, absence of menstruation, fine hair on the body (lanugo), cold hands, brittle hair. Denial is often present. Highest mortality rate of psychiatric disorders. Urgent multidisciplinary care: nutritionist, psychiatrist, internist. Hospitalization if severe." * Q: "Vitamin D deficiency, who is affected?" * A: "Very common. Role: bones, immunity, muscles, mood. People at risk: elderly, dark skin, veiled, obese, low sun exposure, malabsorption. Deficiency symptoms: fatigue, bone/muscle pain, depression, frequent infections, brittle hair. Serious complications: rickets (child), osteomalacia (adult). Dosage: blood test. Supplementation: vitamin D3. Sources: sun, oily fish, eggs." * Q: "Iron deficiencies, why are they so common?" * A: "The most widespread nutritional deficiency worldwide. At-risk populations: menstruating women, children, vegetarians, pregnant women, intensive athletes. Causes: blood loss (menstruation, gastrointestinal bleeding), insufficient intake, malabsorption. Symptoms: fatigue, paleness, shortness of breath on exertion, palpitations, brittle/ridged nails, pica (craving to eat dirt/ice). Diagnosis: low ferritin. Treatment: iron + vitamin C supplements. Sources: red meat, legumes." * Q: "Protein malnutrition, also in developed countries?" * A: "Yes. Kwashiorkor (protein deficiency) isn't just found in poor countries. It also affects the elderly (sarcopenia), anorexics, alcoholics, people with chronic illnesses, and those on extreme diets. Signs include: edema (swollen abdomen), muscle wasting, brittle/depigmented hair, slow wound healing, low immunity, and fatigue. Albumin levels are low. Treatment involves gradual refeeding and protein supplements. Prevention includes a varied diet and 1-1.2g of protein/kg/day for adults." * Q: "Ketogenic diet, medical risks?" * A: "Very low-carb, high-fat diet. Initial effects: fatigue, nausea, headaches (keto flu). Risks: vitamin/fiber deficiencies, constipation, kidney stones, high cholesterol, liver damage, muscle loss. Contraindicated: insulin-dependent diabetics, kidney/liver failure, eating disorders. Medically used: refractory epilepsy. Always consult a doctor first. Not suitable for everyone long-term." Addictions * Q: "Alcoholism, how to recognize addiction?" * A: "Addiction if: need to increase doses, withdrawal symptoms (tremors, sweating, anxiety without alcohol), inability to control consumption, neglect of activities/relationships, continuation despite consequences. Risk thresholds: men >21 drinks/week, women >14. Complications: cirrhosis, cancers (throat, esophagus, liver, breast), neuropathies, heart disease, dementia, accidents. Abrupt withdrawal is DANGEROUS (seizures, delirium tremens). Help: doctor, addiction treatment center, AA groups." * Q: "Tobacco, what are the effects on each organ?" * R: "Lungs: COPD, emphysema, cancers, infections. Heart/vessels: heart attack, stroke, arteritis, hypertension. Mouth/throat: cancers, periodontitis, bad breath. Kidneys/bladder: cancers. Pancreas: cancer. Esophagus/stomach: reflux, cancers. Skin: accelerated aging. Fertility: reduced in men and women. Pregnancy: prematurity, low birth weight, sudden infant death syndrome. No organ spared. Stop immediate benefits in the long term." * Q: "Is cannabis really harmless?" * A: "No. Risks: psychosis (especially high doses/early use/predisposition), anxiety, depression, decreased motivation/memory, psychological dependence (10% of users, 50% daily), chronic bronchitis if smoked, cannabis syndrome (cyclic vomiting), dangerous driving, drug interactions. Adolescent use is particularly risky (brain development). Supervised medical use exists (pain, epilepsy, nausea from chemotherapy)." * Q: "Cocaine, effects on the heart?" * R: "Serious cardiovascular effects even in young people. Immediate: tachycardia, hypertension, coronary spasm. Complications: myocardial infarction (main cause in young people without risk factors), serious arrhythmias, stroke, aortic dissection, sudden death. Repeated: dilated cardiomyopathy, heart failure. Any chest pain after cocaine use = cardiological emergency. Combination of alcohol + cocaine (cocaethylene): increased cardiac toxicity." * Q: "Opioid withdrawal symptoms?" * A: "Painful but rarely fatal (unlike alcohol/benzodiazepines). Symptoms begin 8-24 hours after the last dose: anxiety, agitation, insomnia, yawning, watery eyes, runny nose. Then: muscle pain, cramps, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, sweating, chills, tachycardia, dilated pupils, goosebumps. Peak 48-72 hours, improvement 7-10 days. Medical treatment (methadone, buprenorphine) facilitates withdrawal. Never stop alone." * Q: "Pathological gambling, a true addiction?" * A: "YES. Recognized behavioral addiction (non-substance). Criteria: constant preoccupation with gambling, need to bet more, irritability if attempts to stop, gambling to escape problems, lying to those around you, jeopardizing financial/relational/professional situation. Comorbidities: depression, anxiety, ADHD, addictions. Treatment: CBT (very effective), support groups (Gamblers Anonymous), antidepressants. Helpline: 0 974 75 13 13." Reproductive Health * Q: "Male infertility, causes?" * A: "40% of infertility in couples is attributable to the man. Causes: spermogram abnormalities (oligospermia, azoospermia, asthenospermia, teratospermia), varicocele, infections (chlamydia, mumps), hormones, obstruction of the vas deferens, cryptorchidism (undescended testicle), genetic causes (Klinefelter syndrome, Y microdeletion), chemotherapy, excessive heat (exposed occupations), tobacco/alcohol/drugs. Assessment: spermogram, hormones, ultrasound, genetics. Treatments: IUI, IVF, ICSI." * Q: "IVF, how does it work?" * A: "In Vitro Fertilization. Steps: ovarian stimulation (hormone injections), egg retrieval (anesthesia), laboratory fertilization (conventional IVF or ICSI if there is a sperm problem), embryo culture for 3-5 days, embryo transfer to the uterus, pregnancy test 15 days later. Success rate: 30-40% per attempt (<35 years old). Complications: ovarian hyperstimulation, multiple pregnancies. In France: covered by Social Security up to age 43, 4 attempts." * Q: "Is an ectopic pregnancy dangerous?" * A: "SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Embryo developing outside the uterus (fallopian tube 95%). Symptoms: unilateral abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding, positive pregnancy test. Ruptured fallopian tube: sudden intense pain, malaise, internal bleeding. Risk factors: history of ectopic pregnancy, salpingitis, IUD, fallopian tube surgery, smoking. Diagnosis: ultrasound + βHCG. Treatment: methotrexate (medical) or surgery. Emergency if pain occurs during pregnancy." * Q: "Postpartum endometritis, signs?" * A: "Uterine infection after delivery. Occurs 2-10 days postpartum. Symptoms: fever, uterine pain, heavy/foul-smelling lochia, soft and painful uterus. Risk factors: cesarean section, prolonged rupture of membranes, multiple examinations, obstetric maneuvers. Diagnosis: clinical examination + CBC + blood cultures. Treatment: urgent IV antibiotics. Untreated complication: sepsis, infertility. Important postpartum follow-up." * Q: "Is preeclampsia during pregnancy serious?" * R: "Hypertension + protein in the urine after 20 weeks of pregnancy. Mother: headache, blurred vision, epigastric pain, facial/hand edema, seizures (eclampsia = emergency). Baby: growth retardation, prematurity. Serious complications: retroplacental hematoma, HELLP syndrome (liver+platelet+hemolysis), stroke, maternal/infant death. Only treatment: delivery. Strict hospital monitoring. Preventive aspirin for women at risk." Geriatrics * Q: "Falls in elderly people, why are they so serious?" * R: "Leading cause of accidental death in people over 65. Physical consequences: hip fracture (20% mortality at 1 year), head trauma, subdural hematoma. Psychological consequences: post-fall syndrome (fear of falling, restricted activities, depression, isolation). Risk factors: medications (sleeping pills, antihypertensives), balance/vision problems, muscle weakness, environment (rugs, lighting). Prevention: fall assessment, physiotherapy, home modifications, vision correction." * Q: "Senile dementia vs. Alzheimer's?" * R: "Dementia = syndrome: cognitive decline disrupting daily life. Alzheimer's = most frequent cause (60-70% of dementias). Other causes: vascular (multiple strokes), Lewy (visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, fluctuations), frontotemporal (behavior/language), Parkinson's. Important differentiation: different treatments. Assessment: MRI, blood tests, neuropsychological evaluation. No curative treatment. Symptomatic medications. Essential family support." * Q: "Sarcopenia, how to fight it?" * A: "Age-related progressive loss of muscle mass/strength. Begins at 40 (3-8% per decade), accelerates after 60. Consequences: falls, frailty, dependence, mortality. Diagnosis: strength measurement (dynamometer), muscle mass (DXA). Causes: sedentary lifestyle, protein malnutrition, inflammation, hormones. Main treatment: resistance exercise (appropriate strength training) + sufficient protein intake (1.2-1.6g/kg/day). Vitamin D is important. It's never too late to start." * Q: "Why is dehydration common in the elderly?" * A: "Elderly people are less sensitive to thirst, have less body water, reduced kidney function, take diuretics, and may experience dementia (forgetting to drink) and dependency (requiring assistance). Misleading signs include confusion, falls, fever, constipation, dark urine, hypotension, and skin that remains wrinkled when folded. Complications include acute renal failure, stroke, and infections. Prevention involves drinking water regularly, even without thirst (1.5L/day), monitoring admissions and discharges, and vigilant caregivers." * Q: "Slippery syndrome in the elderly?" * R: "Severe geriatric syndrome. Elderly person 'lets themselves die' progressively after a triggering event (loss of spouse, hospitalization, fall, bereavement). Symptoms: refusal to eat/drink, mutism, total withdrawal, bedridden, infections, swallowing difficulties, rapid death if not treated. Treatment: urgent comprehensive care (palliative care team, geriatric psychiatry, social services), restoring meaning to life, treating depression. Prevention: maintaining social connections, projects." * Q: "Pressure ulcers, how to prevent them?" * R: "Skin lesion from prolonged pressure (bony areas: sacrum, heels, hips). Bedridden/chairbound individuals, malnourished, incontinent, with sensory disturbances. Stages: persistent redness, skin opening, necrosis, deep involvement. Prevention: repositioning every 2-3 hours, pressure-relieving mattress/cushion, skin hygiene, optimal nutrition, hydration, early mobilization. Treatment: complete offloading, specialized wound care, debridement, sometimes surgery. Painful and slow to heal." Additional Skin Diseases * Q: "Acne, how to treat it effectively?" * A: "Inflammation of the sebaceous glands + Cutibacterium acnes bacteria. Types: comedones (blackheads/whiteheads), papules/pustules, nodules/cysts (scars). Factors: hormones, genetics, stress, certain cosmetics/medications. Treatment according to severity: gentle hygiene (no rubbing), topical retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, topical/oral antibiotics, contraceptives (women), isotretinoin (severe forms, liver monitoring + pregnancy). Consult a dermatologist if persistent/scars." * Q: "Shingles, who is it contagious to?" * A: "Reactivation of the varicella-zoster virus (dormant in nerve ganglia). Painful unilateral vesicular rash following a nerve (frequent chest band). Intense neuropathic pain before/during/after. Contagious ONLY to people who have never had varicella (can give them varicella, not shingles). Immunocompromised people, infants, non-immune pregnant women = should be protected. Treatment: early antivirals (<72h), analgesics. Shingles vaccine recommended for >65 years." * Q: "Vitiligo, cause and treatment?" * A: "Destruction of melanocytes (skin pigment cells): depigmented white patches. Probably autoimmune. Areas: face, hands, wrists, knees, genitals. Unpredictable evolution (stabilization or spread). Not contagious. Significant psychological impact. Treatments: topical corticosteroids, tacrolimus, phototherapy (narrowband UVB), excimer laser, depigmentation of healthy areas (extreme cases), cosmetic camouflage. Variable response. Michael Jackson had vitiligo." * Q: "Is rosacea different from acne?" * A: "YES. Rosacea: persistent facial redness (cheeks, nose, forehead, chin), visible blood vessels, hot flashes, papules/pustules without comedones, red eyes. Aggravating factors: sun, alcohol, spicy foods, heat, stress, exercise. Advanced stage rhinophyma (thickening of the nose = rhinophyma). Topical treatments: metronidazole, azelaic acid, ivermectin. Systemic: doxycycline. Vascular laser. Sun protection is essential. No cure, but control is possible." Genetic Diseases * Q: "Down syndrome, prenatal screening?" * R: "Chromosome 21 in triplicate. Risk increases with maternal age. Combined screening for T1 (11-13 weeks gestation): nuchal translucency ultrasound + maternal serum markers + age. If high risk: NIPT (fetal DNA from maternal blood, very reliable) then confirmation by amniocentesis if positive. Characteristics: variable intellectual disability, heart defects (50%), hypotonia, typical physical traits. Life expectancy: 60 years currently. Improved school/social inclusion." * Q: "Turner syndrome, who is affected?" * R: "Chromosomal abnormality in girls: missing X chromosome (45,X). Frequency 1/2500 girls. Signs: short stature, absence of spontaneous puberty (non-functional ovaries), webbed neck (pterygium colli), broad chest, low-set ears, cardiac/renal malformations. Diagnosis: karyotype. Treatment: growth hormone from childhood, estrogens at puberty, lifelong hormone replacement. Fertility: possibility of egg donation. Intelligence is generally normal." * Q: "Hemophilia, only in boys?" * R: "Not exclusively, but primarily. X-linked transmission: affected boys (XY), carrier girls (XX, possible moderate symptoms). Affected female: possible if hemophiliac father + carrier mother (rare). Hemophilia A (factor VIII, 80%), B (factor IX, 20%), C (factor XI, rare, autosomal). Diagnosis: coagulation time, factor dosage. Prenatal diagnosis possible. Treatment: preventive/curative clotting factor injections. Gene therapy shows promise." * Q: “Neurofibromatosis type 1, signs?” * R: "Common genetic disease (1/3000). Autosomal dominant. Diagnostic criteria: café-au-lait spots (>6, >5mm in children >15mm in adults), cutaneous neurofibromas, axillary/groin spots, optic pathway glioma, Lisch nodules (iris), specific bone lesion, affected parent. Complications: plexiform neurofibromas, hypertension, learning difficulties, rare malignancy. Multidisciplinary follow-up. Symptomatic treatment. Selinexor approved for certain plexiform neurofibromas." Occupational Diseases

questions.question: Tendinitis, different depending on the profession?
questions.answer: Tendon inflammation due to repeated microtrauma. Locations depending on activity: elbow (epicondylitis = tennis elbow, computer users/painters), shoulder (rotator cuff, painters/manual workers), wrist (De Quervain's tenosynovitis, secretaries/musicians), knee (patellar tendon, athletes), ankle (Achilles tendon, runners). Symptoms: pain on exertion, morning stiffness. Treatment: rest, ice, NSAIDs, physiotherapy, injection, shockwave therapy. Prevention: ergonomics, warm-up.

questions.question: Work-related lower back pain, how to prevent it?
questions.answer: Back pain = the leading cause of work absences. High-risk occupations: warehouse workers, caregivers, drivers, farmers. Factors: carrying loads, awkward postures, vibrations, prolonged sedentary behavior. Prevention: training in proper movements/postures, handling equipment, breaks, workstation ergonomics, abdominal/back strengthening exercises, appropriate footwear. Treatment: maintaining activity (avoiding bed rest), NSAIDs, physiotherapy, osteopathy. Chronic lower back pain: a biopsychosocial approach.

questions.question: Occupational deafness, irreversible?
questions.answer: YES. Prolonged exposure to noise >85 decibels destroys hair cells in the inner ear (irreparable). High-risk occupations: construction, factories, concerts, military, farmers. Insidious progression: tinnitus first, then high-frequency hearing loss, then conversational hearing loss. Diagnosis: annual audiogram of exposed areas. MANDATORY prevention >80dB: hearing protection, exposure limitation, equipment replacement. Compensation: hearing aids, recognition of occupational disease. No medication restores hearing.

questions.question: Pneumoconiosis, occupational lung diseases?
questions.answer: Lung diseases caused by dust inhalation. Types: silicosis (quartz, mining/construction), asbestosis (asbestos, building), anthracosis (coal, miners), berylliosis (beryllium, aeronautics), siderosis (iron). Progressive, irreversible pulmonary fibrosis. Late symptoms: shortness of breath on exertion, cough, sputum production. Complications: respiratory failure, lung cancer (especially asbestos). Latency: 10-40 years. Diagnosis: X-ray/CT scan. Symptomatic treatment. Prevention: PPE, medical monitoring, exposure regulations. Sports Health

questions.question: Sudden death in young athletes, can it be prevented?
questions.answer: 80% cardiac. Main causes in young people (<35 years): hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM, leading cause), myocarditis, congenital coronary anomalies, Brugada syndrome/long QT syndrome, accessory thoracic duct (WPW). Prevention: pre-participation assessment (SFMES questionnaire, 12-lead ECG recommended, echocardiogram if suspected). Warning signs: exertional malaise, palpitations, chest pain on exertion, family history of sudden death. AEDs in sports venues. Never ignore symptoms of exertion.

questions.question: Is exercise-induced hyponatremia (during a marathon) dangerous?
questions.answer: Low blood sodium after prolonged exertion. Paradoxical cause: drinking too much pure water (sodium dilution). Long duration (ultra-trails, triathlons). Symptoms: nausea, headache, confusion, seizures, coma, death. Prevention: drink according to thirst (not at regular intervals), isotonic drinks if exertion >2h, avoid excessive drinking of pure water. Emergency treatment: IV saline solution. An underestimated silent killer. Paradox: a slowed-down runner who drinks too much is at greater risk than a fast runner.

questions.question: Rhabdomyolysis, dangerous excessive exertion?
questions.answer: Massive destruction of muscle cells releasing myoglobin toxic to the kidneys. Causes: unusual intense exertion (excessive CrossFit, foolish viral challenge), heat stroke, crush injury. Symptoms: intense muscle pain, weakness, tea/cola-colored urine (pathognomonic sign), nausea, fever. Complications: acute renal failure, electrolyte disturbances, arrhythmias. Medical emergency: hospitalization, massive IV hydration. Blood CPK level (>10x normal).

questions.question: Sports-related herniated disc, surgery?
questions.answer: The majority heal without surgery (90% in 6 weeks-3 months). Surgical indication: progressive neurological deficit (paralysis), cauda equina syndrome (emergency: sphincter dysfunction), unbearable pain resistant to >6-8 weeks. Conservative treatment: relative rest (do not become bedridden), NSAIDs, epidural injections, physiotherapy, osteopathy. Gradual return to sport after remission. Prevention: core strengthening, flexibility, correct technique, avoid hyperflexion and lumbar load. Environmental Diseases

questions.question: Air pollution, diseases caused?
questions.answer: Pollutants: fine particles (PM2.5/PM10), ozone, nitrogen dioxide, sulfur dioxide. Respiratory diseases: aggravates asthma, COPD, infections, lung cancer. Cardiovascular: heart attack, stroke, arrhythmias. Neurological: cognitive decline, dementia, autism (studies). Fetal development: prematurity, malformations, developmental delays. Each additional µg/m³ of PM2.5 increases mortality. Protection: FFP2 masks during pollution peaks, air purifiers, greening, sustainable transportation.

questions.question: Is carbon monoxide poisoning insidious?
questions.answer: YES, odorless/colorless, invisible gas. Sources: faulty heating (boiler, stove), car, closed garage, indoor brazier. Symptoms: headache, nausea, dizziness, unusual fatigue, confusion, drowsiness. Key sign: the whole family/pets affected simultaneously. Emergency: open windows, go outside, call 15/18 (emergency services). Treatment: pure oxygen (hyperbaric chamber if severe). Prevention: CO detector mandatory, annual maintenance of heating and ventilation.

questions.question: Lead poisoning, still a possibility?
questions.answer: YES, especially old paintings (pre-1949 in France), lead pipes, certain industries (batteries, glassworks), contaminated soils. Children are very vulnerable: ingestion of dust/flints. Lead poisoning in children: intellectual/developmental delay, hyperactivity, convulsions (irreversible if severe). In adults: fatigue, anemia, neuropathy, hypertension. Diagnosis: blood lead poisoning. Severe treatment: chelation. Prevention: screening of old housing, precautionary renovations, frequent handwashing.

questions.question: Asbestos, current risks?
questions.answer: Banned in France since 1997, but present in buildings before that date. Inhalation of microscopic fibers. Diseases: mesothelioma (cancer of the pleura/peritoneum, almost specific to asbestos, latency 20-40 years), lung cancer, asbestosis (fibrosis), pleural plaques. No safe threshold. Construction and demolition workers = risk. Asbestos survey mandatory before work begins. If discovered: do not touch, have it removed by a certified professional. Medical monitoring of formerly exposed workers.

questions.question: Extreme heat, heatstroke?
questions.answer: Thermoregulation overwhelmed. Classic heatstroke: elderly/frail individuals, heatwave, temperature >40°C, hot and DRY skin. Exertional heatstroke: athletes/workers, clammy skin. Symptoms: temperature >40°C, confusion, convulsions, loss of consciousness, organ failure. LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY. Immediate cooling (cold water, ventilation, ice packs on armpits/neck/groin). Call 15 (French emergency number). High mortality rate if delayed. Heatwave prevention: stay hydrated, keep cool, avoid going out between 12-4 pm, monitor elderly neighbors.

questions.question: Lyme disease endemic areas?
questions.answer: Global spread. In France: Alsace, Lorraine, Limousin, Auvergne, forested areas in all regions. Ticks are active from March to November, at altitudes of 0-1000m. Prevention: cover-up/light-colored clothing, repellents (DEET, picaridin), full-body inspection after walks (folds, scalp, children especially). Tick removal: tick remover (do not twist, do not burn, do not use petroleum jelly). Monitor for 30 days: erythema migrans = immediate treatment without waiting for a test. In-depth Mental Health

questions.question: Adult Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)?
questions.answer: ADHD persists into adulthood (60% of cases in children). Often undiagnosed. Symptoms: inattention (forgetfulness, distractibility, unfinished tasks), impulsivity (hasty decisions, interruptions), hyperactivity (internal restlessness, need to move). Impacts: work (underperformance), relationships (conflicts), accidents. Comorbidities: anxiety, depression, addiction. Diagnosis: psychiatrist/neuropsychologist. Treatment: methylphenidate (Ritalin) + coaching + CBT. Not a character flaw.

questions.question: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)?
questions.answer: Persistent traumatic reaction following a life-threatening event. Causes: war, assault, serious accident, disaster, abuse. Symptoms: flashbacks, nightmares, avoidance (of places/subjects reminiscent of trauma), hypervigilance (startle reflex, insomnia, anger), dissociation. Lasts >1 month. Treatment: EMDR (very effective, eye movement desensitization), trauma CBT, SSRI antidepressants. Important: untreated trauma = chronic complications. Seek professional help.

questions.question: Is postpartum depression common?
questions.answer: 10-20% of mothers experience the baby blues after childbirth. It's a temporary condition known as the baby blues (3-5 days). Symptoms include persistent sadness, unexplained crying, intense anxiety, irritability, difficulty bonding with the baby, intrusive thoughts (fear of harming the baby), exhaustion, and feelings of failure. Risk factors include a history of depression, lack of support, obstetric complications, and breastfeeding difficulties. Treatment involves psychotherapy, breastfeeding-compatible antidepressants, and social support. Puerperal psychosis (rare) is a psychiatric emergency.

questions.question: Excessive preoccupation with serious illnesses despite reassuring tests. Normal symptoms interpreted as dangerous. Behaviors: repeated consultations, medical internet searches (Dr. Google makes it worse!), repeated self-examinations, reassurance from those around you. Anxiety is maintained by seeking reassurance. Treatment: CBT (cognitive restructuring, exposure without reassurance), antidepressants. Avoid: unnecessary repeated tests (reinforce fear), Dr. Google, excessive reassurance. Recognize the anxiety pattern.
questions.answer: Hormonal Diseases

questions.question: Cushing's syndrome, too much cortisol?
questions.answer: Excess cortisol. Causes: drug-induced corticosteroids (most common), pituitary tumor (Cushing's), adrenal tumor, ectopic tumor. Typical symptoms: trunk weight gain (android obesity), moon face, buffalo hump (nape of the neck), wide purple stretch marks, fragile skin, osteoporosis, diabetes, hypertension, muscle weakness, mood disorders. Diagnosis: 24-hour urinary cortisol, suppression test. Treatment according to cause: surgery, radiotherapy, medication.

questions.question: Acromegaly, adult growth hormone?
questions.answer: Adult growth hormone excess (closed epiphyses). Cause: secreting pituitary adenoma. Progressive insidious signs: enlargement of hands/feet/jaw/nose, coarse facial features, gapping of teeth, deep voice, hyperhidrosis, snoring/sleep apnea, joint pain, carpal tunnel syndrome, diabetes. Late diagnosis (average 7-10 years). IGF-1 measurement + pituitary MRI. Treatment: surgery, radiotherapy, somatostatin analogs. Cardiovascular complications if left untreated.

questions.question: Completely different, confusing name. Diabetes insipidus: lack of ADH (antidiuretic hormone) or renal resistance. Result: very dilute, abundant urine (5-20L/day), constant intense thirst. Causes: hypothalamic-pituitary tumor, trauma, surgery, genetics, medications (lithium). No sugar in the urine. Diagnosis: water deprivation test. Treatment: desmopressin (synthetic ADH) or treat the underlying cause. Severe dehydration if access to water is limited.
questions.answer: Vascular Diseases

questions.question: Deep vein thrombosis (phlebitis), signs?
questions.answer: Deep vein blood clot in the leg (mainly calf). Symptoms: spontaneous calf pain/pressure, unilateral swelling, warmth, redness, visible superficial vein. Sometimes asymptomatic. Major risk: pulmonary embolism. Risk factors: immobilization (long flight, surgery, bed rest), cancer, birth control pills, pregnancy, thrombophilia, obesity, age. Diagnosis: venous Doppler ultrasound. Treatment: anticoagulants for 3-6 months, compression stockings. Emergency if associated pulmonary embolism is suspected.

questions.question: Aortic aneurysm, a ticking time bomb?
questions.answer: Aortic wall dilation >3cm. Often silent. Incidentally discovered on ultrasound/CT scan. Risk factors: smoking, hypertension, age >65 years, male sex, family history. Risk of rupture increases with size (high risk if >5.5cm). Rupture = catastrophic: sudden back/abdominal pain, shock, mortality >50%. Recommended screening: men >65 years who smoke, abdominal ultrasound. Monitoring if small, surgery/endoprosthesis if large. Control of risk factors.

questions.question: Vasospasm of digital arteries in response to cold/stress. Phases: whiteness (vasoconstriction), blue discoloration (deoxygenation), redness (reopening, pain). Primary (idiopathic Raynaud's phenomenon): young women, benign, no lesion. Secondary (Raynaud's phenomenon): underlying disease (lupus, scleroderma, polyarthritis), more severe, may ulcerate. Systemic workup recommended if atypical. Prevention: keep warm, wear gloves, avoid tobacco/beta-blockers. Treatment if severe: calcium channel blockers. Severe cases: IV prostacyclins.
questions.answer: Immunological Diseases

questions.question: HIV and pregnancy, risk of transmission to the baby?
questions.answer: Mother-to-child transmission (MTCT) is almost eliminated with treatment. Without treatment: risk 15-45% (pregnancy, childbirth, breastfeeding). With effective antiretrovirals (undetectable viral load): risk <1%. Protocol: ARV treatment from the beginning of pregnancy, AZT during childbirth, preventive treatment for the newborn, planned cesarean section if detectable viral load, artificial feeding recommended in France. Specialized monitoring of HIV pregnancy. Virological monitoring of the child for 18 months.

questions.question: Organ transplant, rejection for the rest of your life?
questions.answer: Acute rejection is possible, especially in the first year; chronic rejection is progressive in the long term. Lifelong immunosuppressive treatment (cyclosporine, tacrolimus, mycophenolate, corticosteroids) prevents rejection but weakens the immune system. Complications: opportunistic infections, skin cancers/lymphomas, drug side effects (diabetes, hypertension, renal failure). Rigorous lifelong monitoring is required. Graft survival: kidney (85% at 10 years), heart (75% at 5 years), liver (70% at 5 years). Treatment adherence is crucial. Important General Questions

questions.question: Antibiotic resistance, a real threat?
questions.answer: YES, a major global threat. 700,000 deaths/year currently, 10 million projected for 2050 if nothing is done. Causes: overuse of antibiotics (humans + livestock), unnecessary prescriptions (viruses), poor adherence (early discontinuation). Resistant bacteria: MRSA (Staphylococcus aureus), CRE (Enterobacteriaceae), multidrug-resistant tuberculosis. Solutions: antibiotics only if bacterial infection is confirmed, complete the full course of treatment, do not share/save, hand hygiene, develop new antibiotics.

questions.question: Are generic drugs just as effective?
questions.answer: YES, bioequivalents demonstrated. Same active ingredient, same dosage, same pharmaceutical form, same route of administration. Permitted differences: excipients (colorants, preservatives → rare intolerances), appearance. Mandatory bioequivalence studies: absorption ±20% of the original (often <5% in practice). Less expensive (savings for Social Security). Confusion possible if several different generics (different forms). Drugs with a narrow therapeutic index (levothyroxine, antiepileptics): sticking with the same brand is recommended.

questions.question: Do placebos really work?
questions.answer: YES, scientifically proven. Real measurable efficacy: release of endorphins, dopamine, serotonin. Effective for: pain (30-50% reduction), depression, anxiety, Parkinson's (dopamine released), irritable bowel syndrome, nausea. Amplifying factors: pill color, size, price, doctor-patient relationship, patient expectations. Nocebo effect (reverse effect, imaginary side effects) also real. Open-label placebo (patient knows it's a placebo) sometimes also effective. Integrated therapeutic use within the caregiver-patient relationship. Vaccinations

questions.question: Flu vaccine, why every year?
questions.answer: The flu virus mutates every year (antigenic drift). The vaccine composition is adapted annually according to WHO forecasts of circulating strains. Efficacy varies (40-70%) depending on strain concordance. Recommended for: >65 years, chronic illnesses (diabetes, heart/respiratory/kidney failure, obesity), pregnancy, immunocompromised individuals, those in close contact with infants, healthcare professionals. Reduces hospitalizations/deaths among vulnerable people. The flu can kill frail individuals. November is the best time for vaccination.

questions.question: HPV vaccine, for boys too?
questions.answer: YES, since 2021 in France. Protects against HPV-related cancers: cervix, anus, penis, throat, vulva, vagina. Boys protect themselves AND their partners. Gardasil 9 vaccine: 9 HPV types (including 6, 11 genital warts + 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58 cancers). Optimal: 11-14 years before sexual contact (2 doses). 15-19 years: 3 doses. Efficacy >90% if administered before infection. Reimbursed by French Social Security for 11-19 year olds. Public health revolution: expected 80-90% reduction in cancers.

questions.question: Which meningococcal vaccine should I choose?
questions.answer: Main meningococcal groups A, B, C, W, and Y. In France: meningococcal C vaccination is mandatory since 2018 (infants, catch-up vaccination for those under 24). Meningococcal B (Bexsero): recommended for infants, not reimbursed except in specific cases. Meningococcal ACWY (Nimenrix): for travelers to countries with a meningitis belt (sub-Saharan Africa), pilgrims to Mecca, and students in university residences. Meningococcal infection is a life-threatening emergency (death within 24 hours). Vaccines are effective and safe. Consult a doctor based on your individual profile.

questions.question: Can you get vaccinated during pregnancy?
questions.answer: Some vaccines are recommended, others are contraindicated. RECOMMENDED during pregnancy: influenza (every trimester, protection for mother and newborn), pertussis (dTcaP between 20-36 weeks of gestation, protects the infant before their own vaccinations), COVID-19. CONTRAINDICATED (live attenuated vaccines): MMR, chickenpox, yellow fever (except for essential travel to an endemic area). Check vaccinations before pregnancy. For those around you (cocooning): update vaccinations to protect the infant. Consult an obstetrician/travel doctor.

questions.question: BCG (Bacillus Calmette-Guérin): partial protection, especially against severe forms in children (tuberculous meningitis, miliary tuberculosis).
