Mandatory in France until 2007, now recommended for children in at-risk areas (parents born in countries with high endemicity, planned travel, precarious social conditions). Limited efficacy in adults. Effect lasts 10-20 years. Intradermal injection (shoulder scar). Contraindicated in immunocompromised individuals (live vaccine). Tuberculosis remains a major public health problem worldwide despite vaccination.
questions.answer: Travelers' Diseases

questions.question: How can I avoid traveler's diarrhea?
questions.answer: Bacterial/viral/parasitic infection from contaminated water and food. Risk areas: Africa, Asia, Latin America. Golden rule: 'Boil it, cook it, peel it or forget it'. Water: bottled or treated (tablets, filter). Food: avoid raw foods, ice cream, open-air buffets, street vendors. Fruit: peel yourself. Treatment: primary rehydration, loperamide (slows transit), azithromycin if severe. Preventive probiotics are being considered. Hand sanitizer is essential.

questions.question: Altitude, altitude sickness?
questions.answer: Acute mountain sickness >2500m. Cause: hypoxia (lack of oxygen). Symptoms: headache, nausea, fatigue, dizziness, insomnia. Rule: descend if symptoms worsen. Severe forms: pulmonary edema (shortness of breath, pink sputum) and cerebral edema (confusion, ataxia) = EMERGENCY IMMEDIATE DESCENT. Prevention: gradual ascent (max 300-500m/day above 3000m), acclimatization, acetazolamide (Diamox) if rapid. Hydration, avoid alcohol for the first few days. Dexamethasone for emergency severe cases.

questions.question: Yellow fever, mandatory vaccination?
questions.answer: Vaccination is MANDATORY (international certificate) for entry into certain countries (French Guiana, many African/South American countries). Recommended in endemic areas even if not mandatory. Virus transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes. Symptoms: fever, jaundice, hemorrhages, liver/kidney failure. Mortality 20-50% in severe cases. Live attenuated vaccine: 1 dose, lifelong protection (certificate valid for 10 years formerly, now for life). Contraindicated: immunocompromised individuals, infants <6 months, pregnancy (except in cases of risk). Travel medical advice is essential.

questions.question: Jet lag, how to minimize its effects?
questions.answer: Difference between internal clock and local time. More difficult when traveling east (advance clock) than west. Symptoms: fatigue, nighttime insomnia, daytime sleepiness, digestive problems, reduced performance. Strategies: expose yourself to natural light at the right times, adjust meal/sleep times before traveling, melatonin (0.5-5mg) at bedtime in the new destination, avoid alcohol on airplanes, stay hydrated. Jet lag apps (Entrain, TimeShifter) optimize brightness. Recovery: 1 day/hour time difference.

questions.question: Schistosomiasis in African swimming?
questions.answer: Bilharzia: a parasitic aquatic worm found in freshwater in Africa (lakes, rivers, even tourist rivers like the Zambezi, Nile, Lake Malawi, and Lake Victoria). It penetrates the skin during even a short swim. There are no immediate symptoms. Weeks later: Katayama fever (fever, hives, cough). Chronic symptoms: blood in the urine, bloody diarrhea, portal hypertension. Diagnosis: serology 3 months after return. Treatment: praziquantel is effective. Absolute prevention: avoid all freshwater swimming in sub-Saharan Africa.

questions.question: Animal bite while traveling, what to do?
questions.answer: Immediate and prolonged washing with soap and running water (minimum 15 minutes). Antiseptic. Seek prompt local medical attention. Assessment: rabies (animal, country, type of bite), tetanus (up-to-date vaccination status?), bacterial infection (pasteurellosis, capnocytophaga). Post-exposure rabies prophylaxis if necessary (vaccine ± immunoglobulins). Never ignore animal bites in rabies-endemic countries. Continue treatment upon return if not completed. Rabies-free countries: Caribbean, Japan, Australia, United Kingdom, Scandinavia. Palliative and End-of-Life Care

questions.question: Does palliative care mean giving up?
questions.answer: NO. Common misunderstanding. Palliative care = active care focused on quality of life, not cure. Objectives: to relieve physical pain, psychological distress, to support the patient AND family. Curative treatments may be associated. Comprehensive approach: doctors, nurses, psychologists, social workers, volunteers. Planning: advance directives, trusted person. Decision to stop curative treatment ≠ stop care. Dying with dignity, without pain, surrounded by loved ones = every patient's right. Palliative care fulfills this mission.

questions.question: End-of-life pain, can it really be relieved?
questions.answer: YES, in the vast majority of cases. WHO scale has 3 levels: level 1 (mild pain: paracetamol, NSAIDs), level 2 (moderate: tramadol, codeine), level 3 (severe: morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl). Morphine: a key palliative care drug, poorly understood and underused. Does not hasten death if the dose is appropriate. Other pain: neuropathic (antiepileptics, antidepressants), bone metastases (radiotherapy, bisphosphonates). Continuous deep palliative sedation: legally permissible for refractory suffering. All pain must be treated.

questions.question: Pathological grief, when to seek help?
questions.answer: Normal grief: intense sadness, crying, anhedonia, preoccupation with the deceased, variable duration (months to 1-2 years depending on individuals/cultures). Complicated/prolonged grief (>6-12 months): persistent intense pain, inability to accept death, intense avoidance or constant intrusion, difficulty with daily life, feeling that life is meaningless, suicidal thoughts. Risk factors: sudden/violent death, child, dependent relationship, psychiatric history. Treatment: grief psychotherapy, antidepressants if associated depression. Bereavement support groups are helpful. Complementary and Alternative Medicines

questions.question: Acupuncture, scientific evidence?
questions.answer: Traditional Chinese medicine uses needles at specific points on the body. Scientific evidence varies depending on the indications. Recognized effectiveness (meta-analyses): chronic pain (back, neck, knee, migraine), nausea (chemotherapy, post-op, pregnancy), tension headaches. Limited/debated effectiveness: smoking cessation, fertility, depression. Placebo effect difficult to eliminate (sometimes fake needles are just as effective). Generally safe if the practitioner is trained (sterile needles). Reimbursed by some health insurance plans. Complementary, not an alternative to proven medical treatments.

questions.question: Osteopathy, for what indications?
questions.answer: Manipulation of tissues, joints, and fascia. Recognized indications/evidence: non-specific lower back pain (similar efficacy to physiotherapy), neck pain, cervicogenic headaches, functional disorders in infants (torticollis, plagiocephaly, colic). Discussed: digestive disorders in adults, vertigo, pain syndromes. To be avoided: serious pathologies (fractures, cancers, infections, severe osteoporosis). Osteopath: regulated title in France since 2002. Consult a doctor first if you have unexplained pain. Complementary to conventional medicine.

questions.question: Herbal medicine, safe medicinal plants?
questions.answer: Not always. 'Natural' ≠ harmless. Proven effective plants: St. John's wort (mild depression), valerian (mild insomnia), ginger (nausea), echinacea (mild ENT infections), artichoke (digestion). Real risks: drug interactions (St. John's wort reduces the effectiveness of anticoagulants, birth control pills, HIV antivirals), liver toxicity (kava, germander), side effects. Concentrated extracts and supplements are riskier than herbal teas. Always consult a doctor or pharmacist. Clinical phytotherapy should be conducted under supervision by trained professionals.

questions.question: Growing scientific interest. Suggested benefits: improved insulin sensitivity, reduced inflammation, autophagy (cellular cleanup), weight loss. Human studies: promising preliminary results: metabolic syndrome, rheumatoid arthritis, certain cancers (chemotherapy adjuvant). Limitations: studies are still small, short duration, lack of long-term studies. Risks: hypoglycemia, deficiencies, electrolyte imbalances, eating disorders, contraindicated in pregnancy/breastfeeding/type 1 diabetes/malnutrition/certain medications. Always medical supervision if pathology is present.
questions.answer: Additional Neurological Diseases

questions.question: Is a normal life possible with epilepsy?
questions.answer: YES for the majority of patients (70% controlled by medication). Types of seizures: tonic-clonic (grand mal, loss of consciousness), absence (petit mal, blank stare), focal (part of the body). Diagnosis: EEG, MRI. Treatments: antiepileptics (valproate, lamotrigine, levetiracetam). Restrictions: driving (seizure-free interval required), certain professions, swimming alone, alcohol. Seizure in public: lie down on your side, protect your head, do not put your fingers in your mouth, call 15 (the French emergency number) if >5 min. Surgery if resistant. Pregnancy: plan with a neurologist (some medications are teratogenic).

questions.question: Peripheral neuropathy, many causes?
questions.answer: Peripheral nerve damage. Symptoms vary depending on the fibers affected: sensory (tingling, burning, numbness in the feet/hands radiating upwards, loss of balance), motor (weakness, paralysis), autonomic (orthostatic hypotension, urinary, sexual, and digestive disorders). Common causes: diabetes (leading cause), alcohol, B1/B12/B6 deficiencies, medications (chemotherapy, antibiotics), autoimmune diseases (Guillain-Barré), hereditary diseases (Charcot-Marie-Tooth), infections (Lyme disease, HIV). A complete etiological workup is necessary. Treatment addresses the cause and the symptoms of pain (pregabalin, amitriptyline).

questions.question: Restless legs syndrome, disease or invention?
questions.answer: A real neurological condition, underdiagnosed (5-10% of the population). Irresistible urge to move legs accompanied by unpleasant sensations (tingling, burning, pulling). ONLY at rest, evening/night. Relief from movement. Sleep is ruined. Causes: iron deficiency (first thing to look for and correct), kidney failure, pregnancy, genetics, medications (neuroleptics, antihistamines, antidepressants). Treatment: correct iron deficiency, dopamine agonists, pregabalin. Avoid alcohol/caffeine. Quality of life is impacted if left untreated. Additional Heart Diseases

questions.question: Atrial fibrillation, risk of stroke?
questions.answer: Common cardiac arrhythmia (1-2% of the population, increases with age). Atria beat erratically, risk of clot formation (left atrial appendage), cerebral migration = stroke. CHA2DS2-VASc score calculates risk (heart failure, hypertension, age, diabetes, previous stroke, vascular diseases, sex). If score ≥2 male / ≥3 female: anticoagulants indicated. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) or vitamin K antagonists (VKAs). Rhythm/rate control. Catheter ablation is a curative option.

questions.question: Infective endocarditis, bacteria in the heart?
questions.answer: Heart valve infection. Bacteria enter the bloodstream (dental procedures, catheters, piercings, IV drug use) and colonize damaged valves (congenital heart disease, prosthetic valves, rheumatic fever). Symptoms: prolonged fever, fatigue, new heart murmur, embolisms (kidney, brain, spleen), petechiae. Diagnosis: blood cultures (bacteria), echocardiography (adenoids). Treatment: IV antibiotics for 4-6 weeks, hospitalization, sometimes surgery. Antibiotic prophylaxis before dental procedures for at-risk patients. Additional Lung Diseases

questions.question: Pleurisy, fluid around the lung?
questions.answer: Pleural effusion. Causes: infection (pneumonia, tuberculosis), cancer (primary, metastases), heart failure, pulmonary embolism, autoimmune diseases (lupus, rheumatoid arthritis). Symptoms: lateral chest pain increasing with inspiration, shortness of breath, dry cough. Diagnosis: chest X-ray/CT scan, diagnostic pleural tap (fluid appearance, biochemical analyses, cytology, bacteriology). Treatment: underlying cause + therapeutic thoracentesis if large + pleurodesis if recurrent.

questions.question: “Pneumothorax, air around lung?”
questions.answer: Air in the pleural space → lung collapses. Primary spontaneous: tall, thin man, 20-30 years old, ruptured apical bulla. Secondary: COPD, asthma, cystic fibrosis, Marfan syndrome. Traumatic: fractured rib. Suffocating (under pressure) = LIFE-THREATENING EMERGENCY (cardiac compression). Symptoms: sudden unilateral chest pain, shortness of breath, tachycardia. Diagnosis: chest x-ray. Treatment: minor (monitoring), medium-major (aspiration, chest tube). Surgery if recurrence (bullectomy, pleurodesis). Avoid flying/diving until complete recovery. Additional Gastroenterological Diseases

questions.question: Helicobacter pylori, a curable ulcer?
questions.answer: YES. H. pylori bacteria causes 80-90% of gastric/duodenal ulcers. Transmission: oral-oral or fecal-oral, contaminated water. Often asymptomatic. Ulcer symptoms: epigastric (pit of the stomach) pain with meals, nausea, vomiting. Complications: gastrointestinal bleeding (emergency), perforation (emergency surgery), stenosis. Diagnosis: urea breath test, stool antigens, endoscopy. Eradication treatment: triple antibiotic therapy for 10-14 days + proton pump inhibitor. 90% cure rate if eradication is confirmed.

questions.question: Are colon polyps all cancerous?
questions.answer: NO, the majority are benign, but some progress to cancer. Types: hyperplastic (benign), adenomatous (precancerous, risk depends on size/histology). Risk of transformation: adenoma >1cm, villous, dysplasia. Generally asymptomatic, discovered during colonoscopy. Risk factors: age >50 years, heredity, diet low in fiber/high in red meat, obesity, smoking, alcohol. Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP): hundreds of polyps, cancer almost certain if left untreated. Systematic removal during colonoscopy. Colonoscopic monitoring according to results.

questions.question: Diverticulosis, diverticula of the colon?
questions.answer: Small hernias of the colon wall (especially sigmoid). Very common after age 60 (50-70% of the elderly population). Often asymptomatic (diverticulosis). Diverticulitis: inflammation/infection of the diverticulum. Symptoms: pain in the left iliac fossa, fever, bowel movement irregularities. Complications: abscess, perforation (peritonitis), fistula, obstruction, hemorrhage. Treatment for simple diverticulitis: antibiotics, diet. Severe diverticulitis: hospitalization, surgery. Prevention of diverticulitis: high-fiber diet, hydration, avoid constipation. Avoidance of seeds (almonds, strawberries) is not proven necessary. Sleep Health

questions.question: Chronic insomnia, medication or therapy?
questions.answer: Insomnia >3 months, >3 nights/week. Types: difficulty falling asleep, night awakenings, early awakenings. Causes: anxiety, depression, chronic pain, poor sleep hygiene, hyperarousal. RECOMMENDED FIRST-LINE TREATMENT: CBT-Insomnia (Cognitive Behavioral Therapy). Components: sleep restriction, stimulus control, sleep hygiene, cognitive restructuring. Superior efficacy compared to long-term medications. Medications: sleeping pills (benzodiazepines) = short-term use only (dependence, falls in the elderly). Melatonin: useful for jet lag/the elderly. Herbal remedies (valerian, passionflower): modest effects.

questions.question: Parasomnias, abnormal sleep behaviors?
questions.answer: Undesirable behaviors during sleep. Sleepwalking: walking with eyes open, blank stare, unconscious, memory loss. Night terrors: screaming, intense agitation, inconsolable, total forgetfulness in the morning (≠ nightmare). Sleep talking: talking in sleep, benign. REM sleep behavior disorder (RBD): acting out dreams (hits, screams, falls), elderly adults, precursor to Parkinson's/synucleinopathies. Bruxism: grinding teeth (dental splint). Sleepwalking/terrors: secure environment. RBD: clonazepam. Never abruptly awaken a sleepwalker.

questions.question: Child's sleep, recommended hours by age?
questions.answer: Sleep needs according to age: Newborn (0-3 months): 14-17h. Infant (4-11 months): 12-15h. Toddler (1-2 years): 11-14h. Preschooler (3-5 years): 10-13h. School-aged child (6-12 years): 9-12h. Teenager (13-18 years): 8-10h. Adult: 7-9h. Elderly person: 7-8h. Lack of sleep in children: behavioral problems, hyperactivity, learning difficulties, obesity, weakened immune system. Screens = enemy of sleep (melatonin blocked by blue light). Bedtime routine essential. Detailed Pediatric Diseases

questions.question: At what age can ADHD be medication started in children?
questions.answer: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder. Symptoms: inattention (forgetfulness, distractibility, unfinished work), hyperactivity (constantly moving), impulsivity (interrupts, doesn't wait). Present in multiple settings (school + home). Diagnosis: psychiatrist/child psychiatrist, neuropsychological assessment, teacher/parent questionnaires. Treatment: psychosocial interventions FIRST (CBT, parental guidance, school accommodations). Medication (methylphenidate): from age 6 if insufficient, always combined with rehabilitation. No systematic lifelong treatment. Regular reassessment.

questions.question: Childhood meningitis, an absolute emergency?
questions.answer: YES. Any suspicion = immediate emergency (call 15). Infant: fever + bulging fontanelle + refusal to feed + high-pitched crying + hypotonia + photophobia. Child/adult: fever + severe headache + stiff neck (meningeal triad) + photophobia + vomiting. PURPURA FULMINANS: purplish spots that do not blanch under pressure = meningococcal septicemia, DEATH IN HOURS. Inject penicillin G IM immediately if hospital delay >30 min. Urgent IV antibiotic treatment after lumbar puncture (do not wait for results if poor condition). Vaccines available.

questions.question: Type 1 diabetes in children, daily management?
questions.answer: Autoimmune disease: destruction of pancreatic beta cells, insulin essential for life. Often a sudden onset: polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, ketoacidosis (emergency). Management: insulin (multiple injections or pump), multiple daily blood glucose monitoring (meter or continuous glucose monitor), carbohydrate calculation for meals, adaptation of physical activity, management of hypoglycemia (fast-acting sugar always available), school management (PAI). Target HbA1c <7%. Long-term complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) prevented by glycemic control. Normal life with good therapeutic education.

questions.question: Atopic eczema in infants, treatment?
questions.answer: Atopic dermatitis: chronic skin inflammation, very common (15-20% of children). Genetic factors + impaired skin barrier. Symptoms: red, dry, scaly patches, intense itching (sleep disturbances). Locations: cheeks, forehead, scalp in infants; elbow/knee folds in children. Treatment: DAILY emollients (basic treatment, restores barrier), topical corticosteroids during flare-ups (unjustified fear if used correctly), topical tacrolimus if corticosteroid-resistant. Avoid triggers (harsh soaps, wool, sweat, sometimes certain foods). Favorable outcome often (improvement with age). In-depth Oral Health

questions.question: “Adult bruxism, consequences?”
questions.answer: Teeth grinding/clenching, often unconscious at night. Causes: stress, anxiety, malocclusion, sleep apnea, medications (antidepressants). Consequences: excessive tooth wear, tooth fractures, temporomandibular joint (TMJ) pain, morning headaches, jaw muscle pain. Diagnosis: tooth wear, TMJ pain. Treatment: nighttime occlusal splint (protects teeth, does not cure), stress management (CBT, relaxation), TMJ physiotherapy, botulinum toxin injections for the masseter muscles if severe. Underlying cause to be treated.

questions.question: Halitosis (bad breath), underlying causes?
questions.answer: Majority oral causes (80-90%): bacteria on the tongue (whitish coating on the back of the tongue), poor oral hygiene, cavities, gingivitis/periodontitis, poorly maintained dentures, dry mouth. Non-oral causes: chronic tonsillitis (tonsillar crypts), sinusitis, gastric reflux, diabetes (acetone breath), kidney failure (urea breath), liver failure (sulfur breath). Treatment: daily tongue brushing, dental floss, overall hygiene, hydration, treat the underlying cause. Psychogenic halitosis (feeling of halitosis without actual halitosis): CBT.

questions.question: Wisdom teeth, always removed?
questions.answer: NOT systematically. Extraction is recommended if: impacted/semi-impacted causing problems (repeated infections, cysts, pain, destruction of adjacent teeth), position compromising hygiene, orthodontics required. Monitoring is possible if asymptomatic and accessible. Extraction complications: dry socket (intense pain 3-5 days post-extraction), trismus, inferior alveolar nerve paresthesia (mandibular wisdom teeth close to the nerve), infections. A panoramic dental X-ray assesses position before decision. Ideal timing: young adult (incomplete roots, better recovery). Immune and Inflammatory Diseases

questions.question: Vasculitis, inflammation of blood vessels?
questions.answer: Disease group: vascular wall inflammation. Classification according to vessel caliber. Large vessels: Takayasu arteritis (young women), giant cell arteritis (>50 years, possible blindness). Medium-sized vessels: polyarteritis nodosa, Kawasaki disease. Small vessels: Wegener's granulomatosis (ANCA), Churg-Strauss syndrome, Henoch-Schönlein purpura. Symptoms according to affected vessels: organ ischemia, purpura, renal/pulmonary/nerve involvement. Diagnosis: ANCA, biopsy, imaging. Treatment: corticosteroids, immunosuppressants, biotherapies. Serious diseases requiring specialized centers.

questions.question: Amylosis, protein deposits in organs?
questions.answer: Accumulation of abnormally folded proteins (amyloid) in organs. Main types: AL (immunoglobulin light chains, myeloma), AA (chronic inflammatory), ATTR (transthyretin, cardiac/neurological, genetic or aging). Organs affected: kidney (nephrotic syndrome), heart (restrictive cardiomyopathy), liver, nerves (neuropathy), digestive tract. Typical signs: macroglossia (enlarged tongue), periorbital ecchymoses. Difficult diagnosis: biopsy (abdominal fat, organ). Treatment according to type: chemotherapy (AL), anti-inflammatories (AA), tafamidis/patisiran (ATTR). Advanced Stomatological and ENT Diseases

questions.question: Sleep apnea in children, different in adults?
questions.answer: Common, often unrecognized. Main cause in children: tonsil/adenoid hypertrophy (different from obese adults). Nighttime symptoms: snoring, breathing pauses, restlessness, sweating, bedwetting, non-restorative sleep. Daytime symptoms (different from adults): hyperactivity, concentration/learning difficulties (mimicking ADHD), irritability, growth retardation, morning headaches. Diagnosis: pediatric polysomnography. Treatment: adenotonsillectomy (resolution in 80-90% of cases), CPAP if persistent/surgery contraindicated. Screening is important before ADHD diagnosis.

questions.question: Cholesteatoma, a dangerous ear tumor?
questions.answer: Epidermal pocket invading the middle ear and mastoid. Not a true cancer but with locally destructive behavior. Congenital or acquired (chronic otitis, tympanic membrane retraction). Progressive erosion: ossicles (conductive hearing loss), mastoid, facial nerve (paralysis), labyrinthitis (vertigo, sensorineural hearing loss), meningitis, brain abscess. Diagnosis: otoscopy (keratin pocket), ear CT scan. Treatment: MANDATORY surgery (tympanoplasty, mastoidectomy). Recurrence possible. Lifelong monitoring necessary.

questions.question: Facial paralysis, still a stroke?
questions.answer: NO. Peripheral facial paralysis (one entire side of the face, including the forehead) ≠ central (stroke, spares the forehead). Peripheral causes: Bell's palsy (idiopathic, herpes virus, 70% of cases), zoster auricularis zoster (Ramsay Hunt syndrome), Lyme disease, parotitis, parotid tumor, cholesteatoma, trauma. Bell's palsy: sudden onset, early treatment with corticosteroids + valacyclovir, 70-80% recovery. Ramsay Hunt syndrome: ear vesicles + intense pain, less favorable prognosis. Always rule out stroke (neurological examination), parotid gland, and Lyme disease depending on the context. Advanced Gynecological Diseases

questions.question: Early menopause, consequences?
questions.answer: Menopause before age 40 (premature ovarian insufficiency, POI). Causes: genetic (Turner syndrome, fragile X syndrome), autoimmune, chemotherapy/radiotherapy, ovarian surgery. Consequences: menopausal symptoms (hot flashes, vaginal dryness, mood swings, insomnia), early osteoporosis, increased cardiovascular risks, premature cognitive decline, infertility. Hormone replacement therapy (HRT) is RECOMMENDED until the age of natural menopause (51 years) to protect bones and heart. Genetic testing is recommended. If pregnancy is desired: egg donation is possible.

questions.question: Cervical cancer, can it be prevented?
questions.answer: YES, 90% thanks to HPV vaccination and screening. HPV (16 and 18) causes 70% of cervical cancers. Vaccination before sexual contact = maximum protection. Screening: cervical smear (Pap test) + HPV test. French recommendations: Pap smear 25-30 years (2 with a 1-year interval), then HPV test 30-65 years (every 5 years if negative). Precancerous lesions (CIN) are detectable and treatable before cancer develops. Treatment of lesions: conization, laser vaporization. Cancer: surgery, radiotherapy, chemotherapy depending on the stage. Regular screening = saves lives.

questions.question: Vulvodynia, chronic vulvar pain?
questions.answer: Chronic vulvar pain (>3 months) without an identifiable cause. Types: provoked vestibulodynia (pain on penetration, touching), spontaneous vulvodynia (constant burning). Often underdiagnosed, women wander for years without a diagnosis. Impact: sexual life (severe dyspareunia), quality of life, relationships. Possible causes: central hypersensitivity, local inflammation, psychosexual factors. Multidisciplinary treatment: perineal physiotherapy (muscle re-education), CBT, local/systemic tricyclic antidepressants, anesthetic creams, vestibulectomy (selected cases). Questions about Medications and Treatments

questions.question: Anticoagulants, daily precautions?
questions.answer: Blood-thinning medications. Types: Vitamin K antagonists (warfarin, acenocoumarol) require regular INR monitoring, Direct oral anticoagulants (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran) require fixed doses. Precautions: inform ALL healthcare professionals (dentist, surgeon, pharmacist), avoid NSAIDs/aspirin without medical advice (hemorrhage), be careful during activities with a risk of injury, always carry your anticoagulant card. Signs of hemorrhage: blood in urine/stool, significant bruising, prolonged nosebleeds, vomiting blood, severe headaches (cerebral hemorrhage = emergency). Missed dose: follow the instructions according to the medication.

questions.question: Long-term cortisone, side effects?
questions.answer: Corticosteroids are essential, but side effects are dose/duration dependent. Short term: insomnia, irritability, hyperglycemia, increased appetite. Long term: iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome (trunk obesity, stretch marks), osteoporosis (calcium/vitamin D supplementation + bisphosphonates), corticosteroid-induced diabetes, hypertension, opportunistic infections (immunosuppression), gastric ulcer (associated gastric protection), glaucoma, cataracts, growth retardation in children, adrenal insufficiency if stopped abruptly. The minimum effective dose should always be sought. Never stop abruptly if >3 weeks.

questions.question: Chemotherapy, manageable side effects?
questions.answer: Depends on the protocol, but many tools are available now. Nausea/vomiting: 5HT3 antagonists (ondansetron) are very effective. Alopecia: cooling caps for the scalp reduce the risk with certain protocols. Fatigue: appropriate physical exercise, energy management. Neutropenia (risk of infection): preventative G-CSF, isolation if severe, antibiotics if fever. Anemia: erythropoietin, transfusions. Neuropathies: cumulative limitation. Mucositis: mouthwashes, cryotherapy. Fertility: cryopreservation of oocytes/sperm BEFORE if a child is desired. Comprehensive psychological support. Supportive care improves tolerance.

questions.question: Cancer immunotherapy, a revolution?
questions.answer: YES. Immune system reactivation against tumors. Types: checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD1/PDL1/CTLA4: nivolumab, pembrolizumab, atezolizumab), CAR-T cells (genetically modified lymphocytes), monoclonal antibodies (trastuzumab HER2), adoptive cell therapies. Effective against previously incurable cancers: metastatic melanoma, lung, kidney, lymphomas. Immune-mediated side effects: pneumonitis, colitis, thyroiditis, hepatitis (corticosteroids if severe). Not effective for all cancers/patients. Predictive biomarkers select responding patients. Intensive research. Emerging Infectious Diseases

questions.question: Is Mpox (monkeypox) serious?
questions.answer: Zoonotic viral infection (poxvirus family). Endemic to Central/West Africa, global epidemic 2022. Transmission: close skin-to-skin contact, mucous membranes, sexual intercourse, respiratory droplets. Symptoms: fever, swollen lymph nodes, then evolving vesicular rash (macules-papules-vesicles-pustules-crusts), painful. Affected areas: face, palms, soles, genitals, anus. Generally mild (2-4 weeks), severe forms in immunocompromised individuals. Treatment: tecovirimat if severe. Smallpox vaccine is protective. Notifiable disease.

questions.question: H5N1 avian flu, pandemic risk?
questions.answer: Bird influenza virus transmitted to humans through direct contact with infected poultry. Sporadic human cases, high mortality (60% of confirmed cases). Currently ineffective human-to-human transmission (a pandemic would require a mutation). Intensive global surveillance by WHO/FAO/OIE. Pandemic preparedness: antiviral stockpiles (oseltamivir), prototype vaccines developed. Regular outbreaks in Asia/Africa/Europe among wild birds/poultry. Exposed workers: PPE, monitoring. Pandemic risk considered high by experts if a mutation allows for effective human-to-human transmission.

questions.question: Hospital-acquired infections, prevention?
questions.answer: Healthcare-acquired infections (hospital, clinic, nursing home). Main agents: Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), Clostridium difficile (post-antibiotic diarrhea), ESBL-producing Enterobacteriaceae, Candida. Sites: urinary (catheter), pulmonary (ventilation), bacteremia (catheter), surgical. Prevention: hand hygiene with an alcohol-based hand rub (measure 1), contact precautions if resistant bacteria are present, targeted surgical antibiotic prophylaxis, minimum duration of catheter/probe insertion, clean environment. In France: CPIAS monitor infection rates. Patient involvement: request hand hygiene from healthcare professionals.

questions.question: Embedded bacterial communities in a matrix that protects against antibiotics and immune defenses. Resistance 100-1000x greater than planktonic bacteria. Major problem: prostheses (valves, hips, catheters), teeth (dental plaque), chronic infections (otitis, sinusitis, chronic wounds, cystic fibrosis). Difficult diagnosis. Conventional treatments insufficient. Strategies: removal of infected material, biofilm-penetrating antibiotics (rifampicin, fosfomycin), phage therapy (bacteriophages), ultrasound, dispersing enzymes. Intensive research into new approaches.
questions.answer: Practical Questions and Prevention

questions.question: A complete blood test, what does it measure?
questions.answer: Complete blood count (CBC): red blood cells, white blood cells, platelets, hemoglobin. Renal function tests: creatinine, urea, eGFR. Liver function tests: AST, ALT, GGT, alkaline phosphatase, bilirubin. Lipid profile: total cholesterol, HDL, LDL, triglycerides. Fasting blood glucose. Thyroid function tests: TSH. Electrolyte panel: sodium, potassium, bicarbonates. Inflammatory markers: CRP, ESR. Iron status: ferritin, serum iron. Vitamins if deficiency is suspected. Tumor markers if indicated. Personalized assessment based on age, sex, medical history, and symptoms.

questions.question: Cancer screening recommended by age?
questions.answer: Women: cervical smear (25-65 years), mammogram (50-74 years every 2 years), HPV (cervical screening). Men: PSA + digital rectal exam (discussion at 50 years or 45 years if risk, not systematic screening). All: colonoscopy/stool immunochemical test (50-74 years, colon-rectum), melanoma screening (monitoring moles), chest X-ray for smokers >50 years (low-dose CT scan, depending on country). Others depending on family history: genetic testing (BRCA, Lynch syndrome), early colonoscopy if family history of digestive cancers. The benefit of early screening is undeniable.

questions.question: Hand hygiene, correct technique?
questions.answer: First line of infection prevention. Two methods: washing with soap and water (visibly dirty hands, after using the toilet, before meals): 40-60 seconds. Hand sanitizing with an alcohol-based solution (during care, between patients): 20-30 seconds, more effective than washing. WHO 6-step technique: palms facing each other, backs of hands, between fingers, backs of fingers, thumbs, fingertips. Areas often overlooked: thumbs, nails, wrists. Keep nails short, without jewelry. Effective against: bacteria, viruses (influenza, coronavirus), fungi. Not effective if used alone: ​​spores (Clostridium difficile) → washing is mandatory.

questions.question: How to protect your mental health on a daily basis?
questions.answer: Mental health pillars: sufficient and regular sleep (first priority), regular physical activity (as effective as antidepressants for mild to moderate depression; 30 minutes of walking per day is sufficient), balanced diet (gut-microbiota-brain axis), social connections (isolation = major risk factor), exposure to nature (reduces cortisol and anxiety), stress reduction techniques (mindfulness meditation, heart coherence, yoga, diaphragmatic breathing). Limit alcohol, drugs, and screen time in the evening. Seek professional help without shame. Practice self-compassion (talk to yourself as you would to a friend). Advanced Pharmacology

questions.question: Dangerous drug interactions to be aware of?
questions.answer: Common serious interactions: Vitamin K antagonists (VKAs) + NSAIDs (major bleeding), statins + fibrates (rhabdomyolysis), SSRIs + tramadol (serotonin syndrome), metformin + iodinated contrast agent (lactic acidosis, stop 48 hours beforehand), St. John's wort + anticoagulants/birth control pills/antivirals (decreased effectiveness), calcium channel blockers + grapefruit juice (increased blood concentration), lithium + NSAIDs/diuretics (lithium toxicity), quinolones + antacids (decreased absorption). Always report ALL medications (including herbs, supplements) to your doctor/pharmacist. Interaction software is available for healthcare professionals.

questions.question: Medications to avoid for elderly people?
questions.answer: Beers List (potentially inappropriate medications for patients over 65). Benzodiazepines: falls, confusion, dependence. First-generation antihistamines (Phenergan): anticholinergic effects. NSAIDs: renal insufficiency, gastrointestinal bleeding, fluid retention. Anticholinergic antispasmodics: confusion, urinary retention, constipation, glaucoma. Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline): orthostatic hypotension, falls, arrhythmias. Long-acting hypoglycemic agents: severe prolonged hypoglycemia. Antipsychotics: stroke, increased mortality, dementia. Regular prescription review is essential (deprescribing if the benefit/risk ratio is unfavorable).

questions.question: Medications and driving, which should be avoided?
questions.answer: Triangle pictogram on medicine box France. Level 1 (yellow): be careful (mild antihistamines, some antihypertensives). Level 2 (orange): be very careful, consult a doctor (benzodiazepines, antidepressants, antiepileptics, morphine-based painkillers, antinausea medications). 3 (red): DANGER, do not drive (barbiturates, high-dose morphine, some psychotropic drugs). Other: insulin (hypoglycemia), blood pressure-lowering medications (fainting). Always read the instructions. Alcohol + medication increases the danger. Inform a doctor if you are a professional driver.

questions.question: Is a paracetamol overdose really dangerous?
questions.answer: YES. Leading cause of acute liver failure in France. Toxic dose: >10g adult (>150mg/kg), less if alcoholism/malnutrition/enzyme inducers are present. Insidious: initial mild symptoms (nausea), silent liver destruction 24-72h, fulminant liver failure 3-4 days (jaundice, encephalopathy, possible death). EMERGENCY: N-acetylcysteine ​​(antidote) EFFECTIVE if given <24h. Call 15 or a poison control center immediately after excessive ingestion. Even if the patient feels well. Caution: many medications contain paracetamol (do not combine).

questions.question: Biosimilar drugs, even if they are biological?
questions.answer: Biological drugs: complex proteins produced by living organisms (monoclonal antibodies, insulins, EPO, growth factors). Biosimilars: a biological 'generic' version, very similar but not identical (complex molecule, impossible to copy exactly). Strict regulatory requirements (EMA): comparative clinical studies, enhanced pharmacovigilance. Proven equivalent efficacy/safety. Examples: biosimilars adalimumab (Humira), infliximab (Remicade), insulins. Interchangeable according to HAS in many indications. Significant savings for the healthcare system. The pharmacist must inform the patient about substitution. Medical Genetics

questions.question: Predictive genetic testing, should we do it?
questions.answer: A complex personal decision. Advantages: enhanced prevention/monitoring if there is a mutation (breast cancer BRCA, Huntington, Lynch), family planning, reduced anxiety and uncertainty, better understanding of family risks. Disadvantages: anxiety if the result is positive, possible discrimination (insurance, employment), psychological impact of knowing the disease is incurable (Huntington), guilt towards children. Legal framework in France: mandatory genetic consultation before/after, informed consent, results given by a geneticist, protected medical confidentiality. Pre-test genetic counseling is essential. Direct-to-consumer testing (23andMe): unregulated, significant limitations.

questions.question: Genetic diseases, genetic counseling for pregnancy?
questions.answer: Genetic counseling is recommended if: family history of genetic disease, child affected by genetic disease, consanguinity, repeated miscarriages (>2), parental chromosomal abnormality, advanced maternal age. Assessment: detailed family tree, mutation identification if possible, calculation of recurrence risk. Reproductive options: prenatal diagnosis (amniocentesis, chorionic villus sampling), preimplantation genetic diagnosis (PGD: embryo selected before IVF implantation), gamete donation, adoption, accepting risk. No moral judgment: support for the couple in making an autonomous decision.

questions.question: Introduction of corrective genetic material into patient cells. Approaches: functional gene addition (AAV viral vector), genome editing (CRISPR-Cas9), RNA interference (silencing defective genes). Approved drugs: Zolgensma (spinal muscular atrophy, single injection), Luxturna (retinitis pigmentosa), Libmeldy (metachromatic leukodystrophy), CAR-T therapies (leukemias). Clinical trials: sickle cell disease (Casgevy, first CRISPR treatment approved 2023), hemophilia, Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Challenges: immunogenicity, duration of effect, exorbitant costs (Zolgensma €2 million), equitable global access.
questions.answer: Advanced Occupational Medicine

questions.question: Recognized occupational diseases, how to assert one's rights?
questions.answer: France: Social Security occupational disease tables (120 tables of occupational accidents/illnesses). Procedure: declaration to the attending physician (initial medical certificate), declaration to the primary health insurance fund (CPAM) within 15 days of symptoms, CPAM review (3 months), recognition decision. Compensation: 100% coverage of care, daily allowances, permanent disability pension, lump sum if low rate. Not listed in the tables: Regional Committee for the Recognition of Occupational Diseases (CRRMP) if a direct work-related link is proven. Role of occupational physician: prevention, monitoring, workplace adjustments, fitness restrictions. Do not hesitate to consult an occupational physician.

questions.question: Chemical risks at work, protective measures?
questions.answer: Hazardous chemical substances: CMR (carcinogenic, mutagenic, reprotoxic), irritants, corrosives, sensitizers. Exposed sectors: construction (silica, asbestos), agriculture (pesticides), chemical industry, hairdressing (dyes, perms), health (disinfectants, cytotoxic drugs), automotive (isocyanate paints). Prevention hierarchy: substitution (less hazardous product), collective protection (ventilation, source capture), personal protection (gloves, masks, gowns) AS A LAST RESORT. Safety data sheets (SDS) are mandatory. Occupational exposure limit values ​​(OELs). Biological monitoring of exposure.

questions.question: When does work-related stress become pathological?
questions.answer: Acute stress is normal and useful (performance). It becomes pathological if chronic, overwhelming resources. Consequences: burnout (exhaustion), generalized anxiety, depression, sleep disorders, cardiovascular diseases (heart attack, hypertension), musculoskeletal disorders, weakened immune system. Work-related factors: quantitative/qualitative overload, lack of autonomy, low social support, job insecurity, value conflicts, harassment. Screening tools: Maslach Burnout Inventory, Karasek questionnaire (demand/control/support), Siegrist (effort/reward). Occupational physician, occupational psychologist, HR department, unions = resources.

questions.question: Past asbestos exposure, what to do now?
questions.answer: Asbestos is a known carcinogen, with no safe threshold. It has a very long latency period (20-50 years). Workers exposed before 1997: construction, shipyards, industry, automotive. Post-occupational monitoring: general practitioner or pulmonologist, chest CT scan every 5 years if exposure is significant (post-exposure/post-occupational monitoring program). Reportable diseases: mesothelioma (pleural cancer), bronchopulmonary cancer, asbestosis, pleural plaques. Occupational disease declaration possible even for retirees. FIVA (Asbestos Victims Compensation Fund): compensation even without formal recognition of occupational disease. Rare Dermatological Diseases

questions.question: Bullous pemphigoid, blister disease?
questions.answer: Autoimmune bullous dermatosis, affecting elderly individuals (>70 years). Antibodies attack the dermis-epidermis junction. Symptoms: large, tense blisters on normal or erythematous skin, intense itching preceding blisters, mucosal involvement is rare (difference to pemphigus). Diagnosis: skin biopsy (direct immunofluorescence), serum antibodies (anti-BP180/230). Treatment: very potent topical corticosteroids (clobetasol) or systemic corticosteroids, immunosuppressants. Chronic disease requiring long-term treatment. Mortality is linked to comorbidities in the elderly and treatment side effects.

questions.question: Ichthyosis, scaly skin?
questions.answer: Genetic disease group: skin keratinization abnormality (excessive desquamation, thickened scaly skin). Types: ichthyosis vulgaris (common, mild, dominant, filaggrin mutation), X-linked ichthyosis (boys, steroid sulfatase deficiency), lamellar ichthyosis (rare, recessive, severe), ichthyosiform erythroderma. Symptoms: dry skin, white/grey/brown scales, thickening of palms/soles, itching, superinfections. Treatment: very rich emollients daily, keratolytics (urea, salicylic acid), baths, systemic retinoids in severe cases. Lifelong disease, not curable. Significant psychological impact.

questions.question: Abnormal accumulation of mast cells (immune cells) in organs. Cutaneous (urticaria pigmentosa): reddish-brown macules, Darier's sign (redness/swelling upon scratching). Systemic: bone marrow, liver, spleen, digestive tract. Symptoms: flushing (redness), urticaria, pruritus, diarrhea, malaise, anaphylactoid shock (triggered by: heat, cold, alcohol, certain medications, stress, stings). Elevated serum tryptase. Diagnosis: skin/bone marrow biopsy, KIT D816V mutation. Treatment: antihistamines, auto-injectable adrenaline (always have on hand), avoidance of triggers, imatinib/midostaurin for severe forms.
questions.answer: Legal and Forensic Medicine

questions.question: Sudden infant death syndrome, can it be prevented?
questions.answer: Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS): unexpected death <1 year with no cause found after autopsy. Frequency: 0.3-0.5/1000 births. Peak: 2-4 months. Risk factors: prone/side sleeping position, soft surface, excessive bedding, passive smoking, overheating, bed-sharing with adults. ESSENTIAL prevention: back sleeping (50% reduction in SIDS since recommendation), firm surface suitable for infants, room temperature 16-20°C, pacifier (protective effect), breastfeeding (protective), avoid tobacco. Home apnea monitors: not recommended for SIDS prevention without selection.

questions.question: Poisoning, clinical signs depending on the toxin?
questions.answer: Toxidrological syndromes according to mechanism. Cholinergic syndrome (organophosphates, insecticides): miosis, bradycardia, hyperhidrosis, bronchospasm, diarrhea, convulsions. Anticholinergic syndrome (atropine, antihistamines): mydriasis, tachycardia, dry mouth, urinary retention, hyperthermia, hallucinations, confusion. Opioid syndrome: punctiform miosis, bradypnea, coma, naloxone antidote. Serotonin syndrome (SSRI+tramadol): agitation, hyperthermia, myoclonus, diarrhea. CO poisoning: headache, nausea, confusion (cherry skin, late and rare). Poison Control Center: 0800 59 59 59 (free). Pediatric Oral and Dental Diseases

questions.question: Baby's first teeth, when and how?
questions.answer: Teething: lower central incisors (6-10 months), upper central incisors (8-12 months), lateral incisors (9-13 months), first molars (13-19 months), canines (16-23 months), second molars (25-33 months). 20 baby teeth in total. Symptoms of eruption: excessive salivation, irritability, swollen gums, need to bite, slight fever (not >38°C, otherwise another cause). Relief: chilled teething ring, gums massaged with a clean finger. Avoid lidocaine gum gels (infants). First dental consultation as soon as the first tooth erupts or at 1 year of age.

questions.question: Cavities in baby teeth, how to treat them if they fall out?
questions.answer: YES, absolutely treat. Important reasons: pain (child is suffering), infections that can affect developing permanent teeth (affecting future dentition), abscesses (spread of infection, possible sepsis), premature tooth loss (lack of space for permanent teeth, malocclusion), feeding/growth difficulties, speech disorders. Prevention: fluoride from 6 months (drops/tablets depending on local water), brushing from the first tooth (1000 ppm fluoride toothpaste, rice grain dose), water bottle at night (no milk/juice), limit sugars, regular dental visits. Early childhood caries = a public health problem. Pediatric Cardiovascular Diseases

questions.question: Are congenital heart defects common?
questions.answer: First congenital malformation (8/1000 births). Frequent types: ventricular septal defect (VSD, hole between ventricles, spontaneous closure possible), patent ductus arteriosus (PDA, premature infants++), pulmonary/aortic stenosis, tetralogy of Fallot (cyanotic), transposition of the great arteries (neonatal emergency). Screening: fetal morphology ultrasound, neonatal oximetry (mandatory in France). Symptoms: heart murmur, cyanosis, feeding difficulties, insufficient growth, shortness of breath. Treatments: medication, interventional catheterization, cardiac surgery. Prognosis transformed by modern surgery.

questions.question: A complication of untreated or poorly treated streptococcal pharyngitis. Developed countries: rare thanks to antibiotics. Developing countries: still frequent, causes valvular heart disease. Occurs 2-4 weeks after group A streptococcal pharyngitis. Jones criteria: migratory polyarthritis (changing inflamed joints), carditis (inflammation of the heart, mitral/aortic valves), Sydenham's chorea (involuntary movements), erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules. Treatment: penicillin (eradicate streptococcus), anti-inflammatories, monthly prophylactic penicillin for years (prevent recurrence of carditis). Screening/treatment of streptococcal pharyngitis prevents rheumatic fever.
questions.answer: Advanced Nutrition

questions.question: Gut microbiota, impact on overall health?
questions.answer: 100 trillion gut bacteria (10 times the body's cells). Roles: digestion (fiber, vitamins K/B12), immunity (70% of immune cells in the gut), gut-brain axis (serotonin, mood, behavior), pathogen protection, drug metabolism. Associated dysbiosis (imbalance): obesity, type 2 diabetes, IBD, IBS, allergies, autism, depression, Alzheimer's. Disrupting factors: antibiotics, low-fiber/ultra-processed diet, stress, sedentary lifestyle. Improvement: fiber (prebiotics), fermented foods (yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut), dietary diversity, exercise. Probiotics: proven benefits, specific indications.

questions.question: What is an anti-inflammatory diet?
questions.answer: Chronic low-grade inflammation is implicated in: cardiovascular diseases, cancers, diabetes, Alzheimer's, autoimmune diseases. Pro-inflammatory foods: refined sugars, trans fats, vegetable oils rich in omega-6 (sunflower, corn), processed meats, alcohol, ultra-processed foods. Anti-inflammatory foods: omega-3 (oily fish, nuts, flax seeds), colorful vegetables/fruits (polyphenols, antioxidants), spices (turmeric, ginger), extra-virgin olive oil, legumes, fermented foods. The Mediterranean diet is the best proven anti-inflammatory model. Tobacco, stress, and a sedentary lifestyle are also pro-inflammatory.

questions.question: Vegetarianism/veganism, deficiencies to watch out for?
questions.answer: Vegetarian (no meat/fish): monitor iron (non-heme iron is less absorbed, combine with vitamin C), zinc, omega-3 (EPA/DHA, sources: microalgae, fortified eggs). Vegan (no animal products): vitamin B12 supplementation is MANDATORY (non-negotiable, serious neurological deficiency), monitor vitamin D, calcium (legumes, tofu, broccoli, calcium-rich water), iodine (algae or supplementation), iron, zinc, omega-3, complete proteins (combination of grains and legumes). Well-planned: healthy at any age (position of nutrition academies). Vegan children: rigorous dietary monitoring is essential. Sexual Health

questions.question: Is erectile dysfunction always organic?
questions.answer: NO. Causes: psychological (performance anxiety, depression, stress, relationship conflicts, 30-40%), organic (vascular++, neurological, hormonal, medications), mixed (often). Cardiovascular warning sign: erectile dysfunction can precede a heart attack 3-5 years (same mechanism: atherosclerosis of the penile/coronary arteries). Assessment: blood glucose, lipids, testosterone, blood pressure. Treatments: PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil/Viagra, tadalafil/Cialis), psychotherapy/sex therapy, intracavernosal injections, vacuum erection devices, prostheses. Lifestyle: exercise, smoking cessation, weight loss. Consult without shame.

questions.question: Vaginismus, is treatment possible?
questions.answer: Uncontrollable involuntary contraction of the pelvic floor muscles making penetration impossible/painful. Primary (never had penetration) or secondary (after a normal period). Causes: sexual trauma, anxiety, anticipated pain, chronic infections, lack of sexual education. Major impact on the couple. Differential diagnosis: vulvodynia, endometriosis, anatomical abnormalities. Effective treatment: perineal physiotherapy (primary, progressive dilators, rehabilitation), sex therapy/psychotherapy, couples therapy, botulinum toxin injections (refractory cases). Recovery is possible in most cases with appropriate management. Unnecessary shame, recognized medical condition.

questions.question: Male contraception, what options are available?
questions.answer: Current methods: male condom (the only one protecting against STIs), vasectomy (permanent method, reimbursed, effective >99%). In advanced development: male pill (dimethylnortestosterone acetate + testosterone injection, phase III trials), transdermal hormonal gel (Nestorone + testosterone, advanced trials), RISUG/Vasalgel (reversible polymer injection into the vas deferens, India). Withdrawal (coitus interruptus): unreliable method (17% current failure rate). Internal condom (female): usable by men or women. Growing male demand for contraception. Shared contraceptive responsibility is essential. Additional Endocrine Diseases

questions.question: Prolactinoma, a common pituitary tumor?
questions.answer: Prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma (60% of pituitary adenomas). Microadenoma (<10mm) or macroadenoma. Women: amenorrhea/oligospaniomenorrhea, galactorrhea (milk production outside of pregnancy), infertility, osteoporosis if prolonged. Men: decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, infertility, rare galactorrhea, compression symptoms (headaches, visual disturbances if macroadenoma). Diagnosis: prolactinemia + pituitary MRI. Treatment: dopamine agonists (bromocriptine, cabergoline) are very effective (tumor reduction, prolactin normalization). Surgery/radiotherapy if drug resistance/intolerance.

questions.question: Primary hyperaldosteronism, hypertension with low serum potassium?
questions.answer: Excess aldosterone production in the adrenal glands (Conn's adenoma or bilateral hyperplasia). Primary cause of secondary hypertension (5-10% of hypertensive patients). Suspect if: resistant hypertension, spontaneous or diuretic-induced hypokalemia, adrenal incidentaloma. Hypokalemia symptoms: muscle weakness, cramps, polyuria, constipation, paresthesia. Diagnosis: elevated aldosterone/renin ratio, then adrenal CT scan, adrenal catheterization if lateralization is suspected. Treatment: unilateral adenoma → laparoscopic surgery (50% cure rate of hypertension). Bilateral adenoma → lifelong spironolactone/eplerenone. Medical Innovations and Health Technologies

questions.question: Does telemedicine replace in-person consultations?
questions.answer: Supplement, not a complete replacement. Suitable for: monitoring stable chronic illnesses (diabetes, hypertension, hypothyroidism), renewing simple prescriptions, test results, psychiatry/psychology consultations, dermatology (photos of lesions), specialist consultations in underserved areas, simple post-operative follow-up, minor emergencies (urinary tract infection, cold). Unsuitable for: clinical examination required, first consultation for an unknown illness, true emergencies, technical procedures, palpation/auscultation essential. Reimbursed by French Social Security if the primary care physician is registered. Reduced coverage for medical deserts, accessibility for people with reduced mobility.

questions.question: Miniature 3D structures reproducing organ architecture/functions, cultured in vitro stem cells. Types developed: organoids of the intestine, brain (cerebroids), liver, kidney, lung, pancreas, tumors (tumoroids). Applications: disease models (cystic fibrosis, Alzheimer's, COVID-19), personalized drug testing (patient tumoroid tested before chemotherapy), embryonic development studies, replacement of animal experimentation. Limitations: limited size (no vascularization), incomplete functions, inter-batch variability. Future: personalized pharmacology, regenerative medicine, partial replacement of laboratory animals.
questions.answer: Advanced Psychiatry

questions.question: Screen addiction in children, criteria?
questions.answer: Not yet officially diagnosed under the DSM-5 (gaming disorder currently being recognized). Warning signs: constant preoccupation with screens, intense irritability if withdrawal occurs, increased time required for satisfaction, unsuccessful attempts to reduce usage, lying about use, abandonment of other activities (sports, friends, homework), sacrificed social relationships, use to escape problems, academic decline. Risk factors: ADHD, social anxiety, academic difficulties, isolation. Prevention: clear rules from a young age, no screens before age 3, no bedroom, limited time, controlled content. Treatment: CBT, family therapy, alternative activities.

questions.question: Phobia of medical care (iatrophobia)?
questions.answer: Intense fear of medical care, doctors, hospitals, procedures. Types: needle phobia (tripanophobia, very common), blood phobia (hemophobia, single vasovagal reflex: blood pressure drops), hospital phobia, dental phobia (odontophobia, 15% of the population). Consequences: avoidance of care, delayed diagnoses, untreated illnesses, preventable deaths. Treatment: CBT with gradual exposure (very effective), EMDR if medical trauma, relaxation techniques, conscious dental sedation if necessary. Healthcare professionals: compassionate approach, explanation of procedures, patient monitoring, routine local anesthesia. Never minimize fear. Advanced Sports Medicine

questions.question: Doping, long-term health effects?
questions.answer: Anabolic androgenic steroids: pathological cardiac hypertrophy, early myocardial infarction, arrhythmias, sudden death, testicular atrophy, sterility, gynecomastia, severe acne, liver disorders (peliosis), psychological disorders (steroid rage), addiction. EPO (erythropoietin): thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, stroke, death of cyclists at night (high hematocrit). Growth hormone: acromegaly, diabetes, colon cancer. Amphetamines: myocardial infarction, stroke, addiction, psychosis. Repeated corticosteroid use: osteoporosis, immune disorders. No doping product is without serious risk. Anti-doping controls are insufficient to eliminate the problem.

questions.question: Sports nutrition for endurance, strategy?
questions.answer: Key periods. Before exercise (2-4 hours): meal rich in complex carbohydrates, low in fiber/fat, moderate protein, well hydrated. 30-60 minutes before: small amount of simple carbohydrates. During (>1 hour of exercise): 30-60g carbohydrates/hour (isotonic drinks, gels, bananas), 500-750ml fluid/hour adapted to sweat, electrolytes if >2 hours (sodium ++). After (anabolic window 30-45 minutes): carbohydrates + protein (3 :1 or 4 :1 ratio ), rehydration to 150% of body weight lost. Daily: 6-10g carbohydrates/kg/day, 1.4-1.7g protein/kg/day for endurance. Proven supplements: caffeine (performance ++), beta-alanine, creatine (sprint/strength more than endurance). Emergency Medicine

questions.question: Cardiac arrest, life-saving actions?
questions.answer: Every minute counts (10% chance of survival per minute without CPR). Chain of survival: 1) Recognize (person unconscious, not breathing normally) 2) Call 15/18/112 immediately 3) Chest compressions: heel to hand, center to sternum, arms straight, 5-6cm deep, 100-120/min, without interruption 4) Defibrillator (AED): turn on as soon as available, follow voice prompts 5) 30 :2 ventilation if trained (otherwise compressions alone are acceptable). Public training is essential. France: defibrillators are mandatory in public places. The 'Staying Alive' app locates nearby AEDs. Survival is multiplied by 3-4 with early CPR.

questions.question: Drowning, what to do?
questions.answer: Get out of the water WITHOUT putting yourself in danger (using a pole or buoy; do not enter if untrained). Lay the person horizontally (orthostatic hypotension, vasoplegia). If unconscious/not breathing: immediate CPR (5 initial rescue breaths then 30 :2 ; drowning prioritizes ventilation, unlike cardiac arrest). Call 15 (the French emergency number). Hypothermia is common: do not declare death before complete rewarming ('not dead until warm is dead'). Secondary drowning syndrome: monitor for 24-48 hours even if recovery is apparent (delayed pulmonary edema). Prevention: constant monitoring of children (silent drowning occurs in seconds), swimming lessons, life jacket.

questions.question: Malignant peri-anesthetic hyperthermia, a life-threatening emergency?
questions.answer: Rare but fatal pharmacogenetic reaction if left untreated. Triggers: halogenated agents (halothane, sevoflurane, isoflurane), succinylcholine. Genetic predisposition (ryanodine receptor RYR1 mutation). Symptoms: muscle rigidity (masseter then generalized), very rapid hyperthermia (39-42°C+), tachycardia, acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, arrhythmias. EMERGENCY: stop triggering agents, dantrolene IV IMMEDIATELY (specific antidote), active cooling, treatment of complications. Genetic testing of the family afterward. Halogenated contraction test (muscle biopsy) for family screening. Intravenous total anesthesia if there is a family history. Additional Rare Diseases

questions.question: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (EDS), pathological hypermobility?
questions.answer: Connective tissue disease group, mutations in collagen genes/associated proteins. Types: hypermobile (hEDS, the most frequent, no identified genetic marker), classic, vascular (severe, arterial/organ ruptures), kyphoscoliotic. hEDS symptoms: joint hypermobility (repeated dislocations/subluxations), chronic pain, fatigue, soft/extensible skin, slow wound healing, dysautonomia (POTS), proprioceptive disorders. Long overlooked/diagnostic odyssey. Treatment: physical therapy, muscle strengthening (not stretching!), stabilizing orthotics, pain management, psychological support. The French EDS Association is an important resource.

questions.question: Is Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (CFS/ME) real?
questions.answer: YES. Myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome. Complex neurological disease, often post-infectious. Cardinal symptoms: debilitating extreme fatigue (>6 months, not improved by rest), post-exertional malaise (PEM: worsening of symptoms 12-48 hours after physical/cognitive exertion, pathognomonic criterion), non-restorative sleep disturbances, pain, cognitive impairment (brain fog). Often misunderstood, misdiagnosed as psychological. Long COVID shares many characteristics. No biomarker. Treatment: energy pacing, avoid 'push and crash', symptomatic. DO NOT prescribe progressive exercise (worsens PEM).

questions.question: Rare X-linked lysosomal storage disease. Alpha-galactosidase A enzyme deficiency: glycosphingolipid accumulation in organs. Early signs (childhood): neuropathic hand/foot pain (Fabry attacks), heat/cold/exercise intolerance, hypohidrosis (reduced sweating), angiokeratomas (small red skin lesions on the abdomen/navel), digestive disorders. Adulthood: heart disease (left ventricular hypertrophy), progressive renal failure, stroke at a young age. Heterozygous females: variable symptoms (X-linked lysosomal storage disease). Diagnosis: leukocyte enzyme activity, genetic testing. Enzyme replacement therapy (agalsidase) slows progression.
questions.answer: Advanced Preventive Medicine

questions.question: Epigenetics, does lifestyle modify our genes?
questions.answer: Epigenetics: modifications to gene expression without changing the DNA sequence. Mechanisms: DNA methylation, histone modifications, non-coding RNAs. Lifestyle influences the epigenome: smoking (methylation of tumor suppressor genes), diet (folates, polyphenols, B vitamins modify methylation), exercise (improves the expression of metabolic/anti-inflammatory genes), chronic stress (modifies the HPA axis, possible transmission to the next generation). Transgenerational epigenetic heritability: stress/famine in grandparents can influence the health of grandchildren (Amsterdam famine studies, descendants of Holocaust survivors). Good news: reversible modifications with behavioral changes.

questions.question: Light pollution, effects on health?
questions.answer: Artificial light exposure at night (ALAN): an emerging public health problem. Effects: disruption of circadian rhythm (melatonin suppression by blue light), sleep disorders, risk of hormone-dependent cancers (breast, prostate: epidemiological studies), obesity, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disorders, depression, impact on reproduction (animals + humans). Sources: LED screens, outdoor lighting, night work. Protection: amber/red lights in the evening (blue light filter), night mode on phones (insufficient), complete darkness of the bedroom, blue light filtering glasses in the evening. Legislation on nighttime lighting: a public health and biodiversity issue. Advanced Digestive Diseases

questions.question: Irreversible inflammation of the pancreas with progressive fibrosis. Main cause: chronic alcoholism (70%), also genetic (CFTR, SPINK1), idiopathic, autoimmune. Symptoms: chronic abdominal pain (often after meals, radiating to the back), painful flare-ups, exocrine insufficiency (fatty diarrhea, malnutrition, weight loss, secondary diabetes). Complications: pseudocysts, strictures, thromboses, pancreatic cancer (risk increased tenfold). Diagnosis: CT scan/MRI (calcifications, atrophy), pancreatic function test. Treatment: cessation of alcohol/tobacco use, pancreatic enzymes, pain management (often difficult), insulin if diabetic, surgery/endoscopy if complications arise.
questions.answer: Advanced Ophthalmological Diseases

questions.question: Retinal detachment, a visual emergency?
questions.answer: SURGICAL EMERGENCY (hours/days). Detachment of the photoreceptor layer from the retinal pigment epithelium. Types: rhegmatogenous (retinal tear, most frequent), tractional (diabetes, sickle cell disease), exudative (tumor, malignant hypertension). Rhegmatogenous risk factors: high myopia, trauma, cataract surgery, family history. Symptoms: sudden flashes of light (phosphenes), numerous floaters, progressive curtain/veil of vision. Detached macula = maximum emergency (central vision threatened). Treatments: cryotherapy/laser (tear without detachment), surgery (vitrectomy, ringing, gas injection).

questions.question: Keratoconus, a disease of the young cornea?
questions.answer: Progressive corneal deformation (thinning, bulging into a cone). Onset in adolescence/young adulthood. Variable progression, may stabilize. Symptoms: progressive myopia/astigmatism, blurred/distorted vision, halos around lights, photophobia, frequent changes in corrective lenses. Risk factors: repeated eye rubbing (forbidden!), eye allergies, eczema, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, Down syndrome. Early stages: glasses/contact lenses (rigid lenses often necessary in advanced stages). Corneal cross-linking: UV-treated corneal reinforcement (treatment if progression stops progression). Intracorneal rings. Keratoplasty (corneal transplant) if very advanced.

questions.question: Uveitis, inflammation inside the eye?
questions.answer: Uveal inflammation (iris, ciliary body, choroid). Anterior (iritis/cyclitis): more frequent, redness, photophobia, pain, blurred vision. Posterior/panuveitis: floaters, decreased vision without pain. Causes: autoimmune (spondyloarthritis, sarcoidosis, IBD, multiple sclerosis), infectious (toxoplasmosis, herpes, syphilis, tuberculosis, immunocompromised CMV), idiopathic. Ophthalmological emergency. Untreated complications: synechiae, cataracts, glaucoma, macular edema, blindness. Treatment: local/systemic corticosteroids, mydriatic eye drops, immunosuppressants if recurrent, anti-infective treatment if infectious cause. Advanced Environmental Health

questions.question: Endocrine disruptors, invisible dangers?
questions.answer: Chemical substances interfere with the hormonal system. Ubiquitous sources: plastics (BPA, phthalates, bisphenol substitutes), pesticides (DDT, chlorpyrifos), cosmetics (parabens, synthetic musks), non-stick pans (PFAS), flame retardants (PBDEs), herbicides (atrazine). Effects: disruption of male/female fertility (sperm, PCOS), early puberty in girls, hormone-dependent cancers (breast, prostate, testicle), obesity/diabetes, neurodevelopmental disorders (autism, ADHD), thyroid. Vulnerability windows: fetus, infant, puberty. Reduction of exposure: avoid heated food plastics, favor organic products, natural cosmetics, filter water.

questions.question: Silent chronic arsenic poisoning?
questions.answer: Inorganic arsenic: IARC Group 1 carcinogen. Sources: naturally contaminated groundwater (Bangladesh, India, China, South America, regions of France such as Limousin), certain foods (rice absorbs arsenic from soils), old wood treatments (CCA). Acute exposure: rare, severe gastroenteritis. Chronic exposure: skin hyperpigmentation (melanosis), hyperkeratosis of palms/soles, cancers (skin, lung, bladder, kidney), neuropathies, cardiovascular diseases, diabetes. Diagnosis: urinary arsenic. Prevention: quality control of drinking water, diversify cereals (not just rice), rinse rice thoroughly.

questions.question: Microplastics, found everywhere in the human body?
questions.answer: Yes, recent findings are alarming. Microplastics (<5mm) and nanoplastics have been found in: lungs, blood, placenta, breast milk, sperm, testicles, and brain (recent studies 2023-2024). Sources: food (packaging, fish/seafood), water (plastic bottles, tap water), air (synthetic textile fibers, dust), and cosmetics (exfoliants). Human health effects: still under investigation but worrying. In animals: inflammation, reproductive toxicity, and endocrine disruption. A 2024 study: patients with microplastics in their carotid arteries showed an increased cardiovascular risk. Exposure reduction: filtered water, less plastic packaging, and natural clothing. Space Medicine

questions.question: Human body in space, what changes?
questions.answer: Microgravity causes profound changes. Muscular: rapid atrophy (2% mass/week without exercise), especially postural muscles. Skeletal: loss of bone density 1-2%/month (accelerated osteoporosis), incomplete recovery. Cardiovascular: fluid redistribution towards the head (puffy face, thin legs), decline in cardiac capacity, return of orthostatic hypotension. Neurological: balance disorders, coordination, altered spatial perception. Vision: spatial neuro-ocular syndrome (SANS): flattening of the eyeball, optic disc edema, refractive errors (major problem on long missions). Immunity: dysregulation, reactivation of latent viruses (herpes, chickenpox).

questions.question: Space radiation, carcinogenic risks?
questions.answer: Outside Earth's magnetosphere: exposure to cosmic galactic radiation (CGR) and solar flares. Types: highly charged particles (HZE), protons, neutrons. Risks: cancer (leukemia, solid tumors, increased risk 3-5% on Mars missions), DNA damage, cataracts (increased risk for ISS astronauts), neurological damage (hippocampal neurons, prefrontal cortex, cognitive and behavioral disorders). NASA exposure limits: 3% additional lifetime cancer risk. Round-trip Mars mission: 2-3 years, significant dose. Protections: spacecraft shielding, solar flare prediction, radiation-protective shelters, radiation-protective drugs under development.

questions.question: Space Adaptation Syndrome (SAS): 60-80% of astronauts in the first few days. Conflict between the vestibular system (which perceives weightlessness) and the eyes (which perceive movement). Symptoms: nausea, vomiting, headache, disorientation, lethargy, lasting 2-3 days, then adaptation. Treatment: promethazine (IM injection, the most commonly used antiemetic at NASA), scopolamine, dexamphetamine. Measures: slow head movements, avoid complex visual activities for the first few days, light physical activity. Gradual vestibular adaptation. Return to Earth: reverse symptoms (gravity readjustment), similar but shorter. Individual sensitivity varies greatly.
questions.answer: Advanced Forensic Medicine

questions.question: Is an autopsy always mandatory after death?
questions.answer: Not systematic. Types: judicial (ordered by the prosecutor's office if death is suspicious/violent/unexplained, forensic pathologist, results to the magistrate), medico-legal (to clarify doubts), medical/scientific (improving knowledge, with family consent, at the hospital). Mandatory if: death is suspicious, death is violent (accident, homicide, suicide), death is sudden and unexpected (infant, young adult), death without a known doctor, death certificate impossible. Virtual autopsy (virtopsy): whole-body CT/MRI, less invasive, additional information, better religious/cultural acceptability. Family can refuse a medical autopsy, but not a judicial one.

questions.question: Forensic toxicology, detecting poisons?
questions.answer: Analysis of toxic substances in corpses or living organisms. Samples: blood (peripheral and central, different concentrations), urine (wider detection window), vitreous humor (stable, minimally contaminated), hair (month/year history, 1 cm = 1 month), nails, bones, organs (liver, kidneys). Techniques: immunoassay (rapid screening), chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS, GC-MS: precise identification and quantification). Challenges: post-mortem redistribution (altered blood concentrations after death), degradation, metabolites vs. parent substance. Substance databases: TOXBASE, TIAFT. Interpretation: forensic pharmacologist. Rare Tropical Diseases

questions.question: Leishmaniasis, a mosquito of hot countries?
questions.answer: Leishmania parasite transmitted by sandflies (small mosquitoes). Forms. Cutaneous (Oriental sore): chronic painless skin ulcer on exposed areas, spontaneous healing possible but scarring, local treatment. Cutaneous-mucosal: destruction of the nose/mouth/throat, disfiguring, South America. Visceral (kala-azar): SEVERE, liver/spleen/bone marrow, chronic fever, weight loss, pancytopenia, extreme fatigue, fatal if untreated. Present in southern Europe (Mediterranean France, reservoir dogs). Diagnosis: serology, PCR, aspiration. Treatment: liposomal amphotericin B (visceral), antimoniates, miltefosine.

questions.question: Lymphatic filariasis (elephantiasis), how?
questions.answer: Paralytic worm Wuchereria bancrofti/Brugia transmitted by mosquitoes. Tropical areas. Adult worm obstructs lymphatic vessels. Acute phase: lymphangitis, orchitis, fever (filarial). Chronic phase: progressive lymphatic obstruction, lymphedema (legs++, scrotum, breasts), elephantiasis (enormous, debilitating thickening of the skin), hydrocele. Blood microfilariae are nocturnal (sample collection at night). Treatment: ivermectin + albendazole (WHO global elimination programs), hygienic limb care (prevents secondary infections). Established elephantiasis: irreversible. WHO program: global elimination by 2030.

questions.question: Yaws, a forgotten disease?
questions.answer: Treponema pallidum pertenue bacterial infection (similar to syphilis but not sexually transmitted). Children in poor tropical countries (Africa, Pacific, Caribbean). Transmission via skin-to-skin contact. Primary stage: painless papilloma (raspberry-like lesion) on the lower limb. Secondary stage: multiple skin lesions, painful bone lesions (osteoperiostitis). Tertiary stage (late, rare): destruction of bone/cartilage (nose, palate). Diagnosis: clinical examination + serology (positive like syphilis). Treatment: single dose of azithromycin (WHO revolution, eradication campaigns). WHO: elimination by 2030. Almost eradicated in the 1950s, resurgence due to laxity in programs.

questions.question: Onchocerca volvulus is a parasitic worm transmitted by black flies (via rapids along riverbanks). It is found in sub-Saharan Africa, Yemen, and Latin America. Dead microfilariae trigger an inflammatory reaction. Skin symptoms include intense itching, depigmentation (leopard skin), and subcutaneous nodules. Eye symptoms include punctate keratitis, followed by chorioretinitis, optic nerve involvement, and irreversible blindness (the second leading cause of infectious blindness worldwide). Diagnosis is made through skin biopsy (microfilariae), serology, and palpable nodules. Treatment involves annual ivermectin (which kills microfilariae, but not adults). The WHO African Programme (APOC) has led to a considerable reduction in blindness. Doxycycline is also used (killing the symbiotic Wolbachia and sterilizing females).
questions.answer: Rare Autoimmune Diseases

questions.question: Systemic scleroderma, hardening of the skin?
questions.answer: Autoimmune disease: fibrosis of the skin and internal organs + vasculopathy. Forms: limited (CREST: Calcinosis, Raynaud's phenomenon, esophageal involvement, sclerodactyly, telangiectasias, slow progression) and diffuse (rapidly extensive fibrosis, poorer prognosis). Organs affected: skin (hardening, retraction, facial mask), lungs (interstitial lung disease, pulmonary arterial hypertension), kidney (scleroderma renal crisis = emergency), heart, digestive tract (dysphagia, malabsorption). Antibodies: anti-centromere (limited), anti-Scl70/anti-RNA polymerase (diffuse). Treatment: symptomatic depending on the organ, immunosuppressants, pulmonary arterial hypertension treatment, nintedanib/tocilizumab (pulmonary fibrosis).

questions.question: Sjögren's syndrome, total dryness?
questions.answer: Autoimmune disease: destruction of exocrine glands (lacrimal, salivary). Primary or secondary (associated with rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, scleroderma). Main symptoms: xerophthalmia (dry eye: burning, gritty sensation, risk of keratitis), xerostomia (dry mouth: difficulty chewing/swallowing/speaking, accelerated cavities), vaginal dryness, dry skin, dry cough. Systemic manifestations: chronic fatigue (very debilitating), arthralgia, neuropathies, kidney involvement, lymphoma (40x increased risk). Diagnosis: Schirmer test, salivary scintigraphy, salivary biopsy (lip), anti-SSA/SSB antibodies. Symptomatic treatment: artificial tears, artificial saliva, hydroxychloroquine.

questions.question: Idiopathic inflammatory muscle diseases. Polymyositis: proximal muscle weakness (shoulders, hips, thighs), elevated CPK, myopathic EMG, inflammatory biopsy. Dermatomyositis: same + typical skin manifestations (heliotrope erythema of the purplish eyelids, Gottron's papules on finger joints, V-shaped rash on the décolleté). Cancer association (adults >45 years): systematic screening (lung, breast, ovary, colon, lymphoma). Myositis with specific antibodies (anti-Jo1: lungs++) guides prognosis/treatment. Treatment: high-dose corticosteroids + immunosuppressants (methotrexate, azathioprine). Interstitial lung disease: a serious complication.
questions.answer: Advanced Bone and Rheumatic Diseases

questions.question: Osteomalacia, bones that soften?
questions.answer: Bone mineralization defect (adult) = soft bones. Causes: vitamin D deficiency (main), malabsorption (celiac disease, bariatric surgery), chronic renal failure (non-activated vitamin D), medications (antiepileptics, bisphosphonates), tumors (rare oncogenic osteomalacia, FGF23).
