Symptoms: diffuse bone pain (pelvis, lower limbs, spine), proximal muscle weakness, waddling gait, insufficiency fractures. Radiological signs: Looser-Milkman lines (pseudofractures). Diagnosis: vitamin D, phosphorus, ALP, bone biopsy (gold standard). Treatment: high-dose vitamin D + calcium, treat the underlying cause.

questions.question: Sterile arthritis triggered by a distant infection 1-4 weeks prior. Formerly known as the Fiessinger-Leroy-Reiter triad (arthritis + urethritis + conjunctivitis). Triggering organisms: enterobacteria (genital Chlamydia trachomatis++, Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, Yersinia). HLA-B27 is common. Symptoms: asymmetric arthritis of the lower limbs, enthesitis, palmoplantar keratoderma, oral erosions, balanitis. Diagnosis: clinical examination + serology/PCR for the infectious agent. Treatment: NSAIDs, antibiotics if Chlamydia is active, topical/systemic corticosteroids, sulfasalazine if chronic. Prognosis is generally favorable (resolution in 3-6 months).
questions.answer: Advanced Hematological Diseases

questions.question: Thrombophilia, hereditary risk of blood clots?
questions.answer: Genetic predisposition to thrombosis. Frequent mutations: factor V Leiden (activated protein C resistance, 5% of Europeans), prothrombin G20210A mutation (2-3%), protein C/S/antithrombin deficiencies (rare but more thrombogenic). Acquired: antiphospholipid syndrome (lupus anticoagulant antibodies, anticardiolipin antibodies). Indications for investigation: thrombosis <50 years without a triggering factor, unusual sites (mesenteric, cerebral), recurrences, women with repeated pregnancy losses, family history. Implications: prolonged anticoagulation if recurrence/high risk, prophylaxis in high-risk situations, genetic counseling, pregnancy (prophylactic heparin). Thrombophilia screening: not systematic after the first thrombosis.

questions.question: Myeloproliferative syndrome: excessive production of red blood cells (elevated hemoglobin and hematocrit), often with increased leukocytes and platelets as well. JAK2 V617F mutation (>95% of cases). Symptoms: facial/hand erythema, aquagenic pruritus (after hot baths, pathognomonic), headaches, dizziness, thromboses (stroke, Budd-Chiari, arterial/venous), hypertension, splenomegaly, erythromelalgia (burning red extremities). Complications: thromboses, transformation to myelofibrosis/leukemia. Diagnosis: CBC + JAK2 + EPO (collapsed). Treatment: phlebotomy (hematocrit <45%), aspirin, hydroxyurea, ruxolitinib (if resistant).
questions.answer: Therapeutic Innovations

questions.question: Senolytics, anti-aging drugs?
questions.answer: Senescent cells: aged cells that stop dividing but remain alive, secreting inflammatory factors (SASP), accumulate in organs with age. Role: accelerated aging, chronic diseases (osteoarthritis, pulmonary fibrosis, diabetes, Alzheimer's, cardiovascular diseases). Senolytics: selectively eliminate senescent cells. Molecules: dasatinib + quercetin (combination, multiple clinical trials), fisetin (flavonoid), navitoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor, toxicity-limiting). Early clinical trial results: idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, chronic kidney disease, type 2 diabetes. Not yet recommended for clinical practice. Potential anti-aging revolution if confirmed.

questions.question: Molecular scissors cutting DNA with precision. First drugs approved 2023. Casgevy (exa-cel, Vertex/CRISPR Therapeutics): approved sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia dependent transfusions (BCL11A gene modification, fetal hemoglobin reactivation). Results: 97% of sickle cell patients without severe crises at 1 year. Clinical trials: TTR amyloidosis (in vivo therapy, remarkable results), leukemias (universal allogeneic CAR-T cells), HIV (provirus excision), hereditary blindness, familial hypercholesterolemia (PCSK9 knockout). Challenges: off-target editing, tissue delivery, exorbitant costs (€2-3 million), global access.
questions.answer: Global Health and Epidemiology

questions.question: The WHO's Global Burden of Disease (GBD) measures DALYs (years of life lost + years lived with disability). Leading causes of death worldwide: cardiovascular diseases (32%), cancers (17%), chronic respiratory diseases (7%), diabetes (2.4%), Alzheimer's (2.4%). Infectious diseases: tuberculosis (1.5 million deaths/year), HIV/AIDS (650,000), malaria (600,000, mostly in African children). Low-income countries: infectious/maternal/neonatal diseases predominate. High-income countries: non-communicable diseases. Ongoing epidemiological transitions in emerging countries (double burden). Mental health: 13% of global DALYs, underfunded. Road traffic injuries: 1.19 million deaths/year.
questions.answer: Advanced Complementary Medicine

questions.question: Mindfulness meditation, medical evidence?
questions.answer: MBSR (Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction, Kabat-Zinn) program: 8 weeks, rigorous studies. Proven benefits: reduction of anxiety/depression (similar efficacy to antidepressants in preventing relapse), chronic pain (reduction in catastrophizing, improved tolerance), hypertension (modest but significant reduction), psoriasis (faster healing with phototherapy if combined with meditation), improved quality of life for cancer patients, insomnia. Brain changes on MRI: thickening of the prefrontal cortex, reduction of the amygdala, modification of the default mode network. MBCT (cognitive): recommended by the NHS for preventing depressive relapses. Optimal practice: 20-45 minutes/day.

questions.question: Aromatherapy, medically active essential oils?
questions.answer: Evidence varies depending on the molecule/indication. Moderate efficacy proven: lavender (mild anxiety, sleep, Silexan oral, approved in Europe for anxiety), tea tree (minor bacterial/fungal skin infections, antimicrobial properties proven in vitro and clinically), peppermint (tension headaches, temporal topical application; nausea, inhalation). Aromatherapy inhalation: post-operative nausea (ginger, peppermint). Insufficiently proven: many popular indications. Risks: toxicity if ingested (never without supervision), allergies, photosensitivity (citrus fruits), drug interactions, contraindications (pregnant women, epileptics, infants). Variable product quality. History of Medicine

questions.question: A dark chapter in psychiatry. António Egas Moniz (Portugal) 1935: sectioned the frontal lobe connections to treat severe psychiatric disorders. Walter Freeman (USA): popularized transorbital lobotomy (ice pick in the orbit, doctor's office, without general anesthesia). 40,000+ lobotomies in the USA 1936-1960. Patients: schizophrenia, severe depression, OCD, but also troubled children, homosexuals, 'hysterical' women. Egas Moniz Nobel Prize 1949 (very controversial). Consequences: profound apathy, destroyed personality, 2-3% mortality. Gradually abandoned with the arrival of chlorpromazine in 1952 (the first antipsychotic). An example of how medicine can cause immense damage without solid evidence. Rosemary Kennedy (JFK's sister): lobotomized at 23, institutionalized for life.
questions.answer: Medical Bioethics

questions.question: Informed consent, is it really being implemented?
questions.answer: Fundamental principle of medical ethics since the Nuremberg trials of 1947 (Nazi experiments). Components: complete information (diagnosis, treatment, alternatives, risks, benefits), understanding (appropriate language, possible questions), voluntariness (without pressure), decision-making capacity (adult, lucid). France: 2002 law (patient rights). Practical limitations: insufficient consultation time, medical jargon, information asymmetry between doctor and patient, implicit pressure ("the doctor knows best"), emergencies (presumed consent). Vulnerable populations: children (parental consent + child's assent), dementia (advance directives, trusted person, guardianship), psychiatry (involuntary treatment). Consent is an ongoing process, not a single signature.

questions.question: Digital health black gold. Challenges. Confidentiality: GDPR Europe, certified health data hosting providers (HDS), pseudonymization/anonymization. Secondary use: medical research (Health Data Hub France), diagnostic AI, epidemiology. Consent: explicit or opt-out? Data ownership: patient, doctor, state, insurance? Discrimination: insurance companies/employers' access to predictive data (genetic diseases, risk factors). GAFAM health: Apple Watch, Google Health, Amazon Pharmacy (massive behavioral data). Digital sovereignty: dependence on American/Chinese clouds for European health data. Tension: utility of collective research vs. individual protection. International governance required.
questions.answer: Disaster Medicine

questions.question: Medical tsunami, specific traumatological features?
questions.answer: Specific tsunami trauma. Mass drownings: see drowning protocol, frequent hypothermia, delayed secondary syndrome. Injuries: blunt force trauma (debris), wounds contaminated by salt water/mud (atypical infections: Vibrio vulnificus, Aeromonas, fungi). Crush syndrome: prolonged compression by debris, rhabdomyolysis, acute renal failure (mass dialysis required). Delayed infections: chronic wounds, necrotizing cellulitis, melioidosis (Burkholderia pseudomallei, tropical areas). Diarrheal diseases (contaminated water). Massive mental health problems (collective PTSD). Destruction of medical infrastructure. International aid: WHO/INSARAG coordination.

questions.question: Chemical attack, medical care?
questions.answer: Chemical warfare agents. Organophosphate neurotoxins (Sarin, VX, Novichok): massive cholinergic syndrome (SLUDGE: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, Gastroenteritis, Emesis + miosis, bronchospasm, convulsions, respiratory arrest). Treatment: massive doses of atropine (up to 20-50mg), pralidoxime if administered early, diazepam for convulsions, ventilation. Mustard gas: delayed skin/eye/lung damage, rapid decontamination, symptomatic care. Cyanide: 'bitter almond', cytochrome C inhibition, antidote hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit IV). Chlorine/phosgene: delayed pulmonary edema, ventilation. Decontaminate the victim BEFORE taking charge (protecting teams).

questions.question: Predominant trauma. Crush syndrome: compression of limbs under rubble, myoglobin release, life-threatening hyperkalemia, acute renal failure. Principles: IV rehydration BEFORE extrication (prevents reperfusion shock), urine alkalinization, dialysis if necessary. Head, spinal (careful handling), and thoracic (pneumothorax, hemothorax) trauma, multiple fractures. Contaminated wounds: tetanus (urgent vaccination), Clostridium infections (gas gangrene), antibiotic prophylaxis. Time frame: 'miracle survivors' found alive >72h rare but documented (minimal hydration for survival). The health system itself is destroyed. Reconstruction of the mental health of the affected population: years.
questions.answer: Emerging and Re-emerging Diseases

questions.question: Long COVID, a recognized illness?
questions.answer: YES, international recognition. WHO definition: persistent symptoms >12 weeks post-COVID-19 infection unexplained by another diagnosis. Prevalence: 10-30% infected (older variants), less Omicron. Symptoms: chronic fatigue/post-exertional malaise (PEM), brain fog, concentration/memory difficulties, shortness of breath, tachycardia (POTS), pain, sleep disturbances, persistent anosmia/dysgeusia, >200 symptoms described. Mechanisms: reactivation of latent viruses (EBV), autoantibodies, altered microbiota, persistent viral reservoir, dysautonomia, neuroinflammation. Treatments: multiple clinical trials (antivirals, immunomodulators). Administrative recognition varies by country.

questions.question: Nipah virus, a future pandemic?
questions.answer: Highly pathogenic paramyxovirus. Reservoir: fruit bats (Pteropus). Transmission: contaminated fruit/date juice (Bangladesh), pigs (Malaysia), human contact. Sporadic annual outbreaks in Bangladesh/India. Alarming characteristics: 40-75% mortality, severe neurological involvement (encephalitis, seizures, coma), possible human-to-human transmission (currently limited), no approved vaccine, no specific treatment (ribavirin under discussion). WHO: R&D research priority list. Vaccine candidates (G protein subunit) in clinical trials. A single mutation event for effective transmission = major pandemic potential. Bat surveillance is crucial.

questions.question: Viral hemorrhagic fevers, is Ebola curable?
questions.answer: Viruses: Ebola (filovirus), Marburg, Lassa fever, Crimean-Congo fever, Rift Valley fever, Dengue hemorrhagic fever. Ebola: mortality 25-90% depending on strain/care. West Africa epidemic 2014-2016: 28,000 cases, 11,000 deaths. Major progress. Ervebo vaccine (rVSV-ZEBOV): approved 2019, efficacy >97.5%, ring strategy (contact tracing). Inmazeb treatment (monoclonal antibody cocktail): reduces mortality from 35% to 9% with early treatment. Complete intensive care improves survival. Reservoir: probably bats (not yet isolated). Transmission: contact via bodily fluids (not airborne). Strict personal protective equipment for healthcare personnel. Comparative Traditional Medicine

questions.question: Native American pharmacopoeia: source of many modern medicines. Striking examples: Quinine (Peru/Bolivia): quinine (malaria). Coca (Andes): cocaine → local anesthetics (lidocaine, procaine). Curare (Amazon): tubocurarine → muscle relaxants for surgery. Ipecac: emetic. Hamamelis (Algonquins): venous decongestant. Devil's claw (Africa/South America): osteoarthritis, pain. Echinacea (Sioux): immunostimulant. Valdoxan (melatonin): influences traditions. 25% of modern medicines: plant origin, including many traditional Native American medicinal plants. Biopiracy: ethical issue (appropriation of indigenous knowledge without compensation). Nagoya Convention: equitable sharing of benefits.
questions.answer: Digital Health

questions.question: Blockchain: decentralized, immutable, transparent distributed ledger. Potential applications in healthcare. Drug traceability: combating counterfeiting (30% of drugs in developing countries are counterfeit), verifiable supply chain (EU serialization pilot). Clinical trials: immutable data (prevents manipulation of results, selective reporting). Secure sharing of patient data: granular consent, sovereign patient data, system interoperability. Refugee medical identity: portable, paperless health record. Automated reimbursements (smart contracts). Limitations: scalability, energy consumption, governance, adoption by conservative institutions, regulation. Pilot projects worldwide, widespread implementation still limited.
questions.answer: Clinical Pharmacology

questions.question: Underreported drug side effects?
questions.answer: Yes, massive underreporting. Studies estimate that 5-10% of adverse effects are reported. The French pharmacovigilance system is comprised of the ANSM (National Agency for Medicines and Health Products Safety) and regional pharmacovigilance centers (CRPVs). Serious/unexpected effects must be reported by healthcare professionals. Patients have been able to report them directly since 2011. Tools: signalement.social-sante.gouv.fr website. Causes of underreporting: time (consultation), uncertainty about causality, fear of legal consequences, forgetfulness. Examples of delayed detection: thalidomide (phocomelia, 10,000 babies), Mediator (valvular heart disease, 500 deaths in France), Vioxx (heart attack). Post-marketing pharmacovigilance is essential: clinical trials are too short/small to detect rare effects. Always report suspected effects.

questions.question: Off-label prescription, is it legal?
questions.answer: YES, under certain conditions in France. Marketing Authorization (MA): validated indication, dosage, and population. Off-label prescription: unvalidated indication, different population (pediatrics ++, children often excluded from trials), different dosage. Legal if: justified patient interest, no authorized alternative, patient informed (consent), explicit mention on the prescription of 'prescribed off-label', documented in the medical record. Frequent examples: adult medications dosed for children, 'off-label' indications in psychiatry (many psychotropic drugs for children), oncology (unvalidated combinations), ketamine for depression before authorization. Physician liability is engaged. Temporary Recommendations for Use (RTU)/early access: ANSM intermediate framework.
